question: Tell me about yourself.

stories.title: The Engineer Builder
stories.situation: I'm Emmanuel Oppong, a Computer Science student at Grambling State University with a 3.93 GPA, graduating May 2028.
stories.task: My goal has been to build real engineering skills — not just classroom theory — through internships, open source, and personal projects.
stories.action: At Ericsson I engineered a Kafka microservice processing 400K+ daily events with sub-200ms latency. Before that at Noon in Abu Dhabi I built an IoT pipeline reducing downtime detection by 40%. Outside work I contribute to facebook/react, apache/kafka, pgjdbc, and kubernetes-client/java — with merged PRs in two of those repos.
stories.result: I'm a full-stack engineer comfortable from low-level C and Go all the way up to React dashboards. I'm looking for a role where I can keep shipping real systems at scale.

stories.title: The Impact Maker
stories.situation: I'm a software engineer and CS student at Grambling State (3.93 GPA, Dean's List, Presidential Scholarship) who has shipped production systems across two countries.
stories.task: Everything I build has to create measurable impact — not just work, but move the needle.
stories.action: At Ericsson I built a React dashboard that cut debugging time from 60 minutes to 10 minutes for a 30-engineer team. I also built MedStroke AI, a stroke diagnosis system piloted at a Louisiana hospital that reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. As NSBE Assistant VP I grew our chapter to 300+ members and secured $3K in company partnerships.
stories.result: Whether it's developer tooling, healthcare AI, or community building — I focus on outcomes. I'd love to bring that mindset to your team.

stories.title: The Lifelong Learner
stories.situation: I'm Emmanuel — a CS student, open source contributor, and engineer who learns fastest by building hard things from scratch.
stories.task: I believe the best way to master a concept is to implement it yourself.
stories.action: I built a container runtime in Go using Linux namespaces and cgroups, and a SQLite-clone database engine in C with B-tree indexing — both from scratch to deeply understand systems. I then applied that foundation professionally at Ericsson building Kafka microservices and at Noon building IoT data pipelines.
stories.result: I come in already knowing the internals of the tools we use, which lets me debug faster, design better, and ship with confidence. I'm excited to keep building at that depth with your team.

question: Tell me about a time you had a disagreement with your manager.

stories.title: Kafka Architecture at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson my manager initially wanted to use a simple REST polling approach to collect pricing events from multiple upstream services.
stories.task: I believed an event-driven Kafka architecture would be significantly better for our volume requirements — 400K+ daily events across 6 topics — but needed to convince my manager without overstepping.
stories.action: I built a quick proof-of-concept showing the latency difference between REST polling and Kafka streaming under simulated load. I presented the data in a one-on-one, framed it around our team's sub-200ms SLA goal, and acknowledged the tradeoffs in operational complexity.
stories.result: My manager agreed to the Kafka approach. We shipped the microservice hitting sub-200ms latency consistently. The system replaced manual SQL workflows entirely and my manager later credited the architecture decision as key to the project's success.

stories.title: Testing Strategy at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, my manager felt 15-20 unit tests were sufficient coverage for the new IoT data pipeline we were shipping.
stories.task: I disagreed — the pipeline handled sensor data from physical devices where silent failures are costly, and I wanted to write 50+ tests covering edge cases and concurrent scenarios.
stories.action: I scheduled a quick sync, showed two specific edge cases that existing tests would miss, and proposed a timeline where the extra tests added only two days. I framed it around the 40% downtime-detection improvement we were targeting — tests were the only way to trust those numbers.
stories.result: My manager gave me the green light. I delivered 50+ unit and integration tests using SpringBoot, JUnit, and Postman. Backend reliability improved by 80% and we had zero production incidents during my remaining time there.

stories.title: Open Source PR Scope
stories.situation: While contributing to kubernetes-client/java, a maintainer (effectively acting as my manager in that context) pushed back on the scope of my PR, saying it was too large to review safely.
stories.task: I disagreed because the changes were tightly coupled — splitting them would have broken the logic — but I needed to handle this respectfully since it's their codebase.
stories.action: I wrote a detailed comment explaining the dependency chain between the changes, added inline documentation to make review easier, and offered to pair on a live review call. I also added more granular commit messages to make the diff easier to follow.
stories.result: The maintainer appreciated the extra context and agreed to review it as one PR. It was merged successfully. I learned that disagreement in open source is won with evidence and patience, not persistence.

question: Tell me about a situation when you had a conflict with a teammate.

stories.title: Kafka Partitioning Debate at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, a fellow engineer and I disagreed on how to partition our Kafka topics. He wanted one partition per topic for simplicity; I wanted multiple partitions per topic to support parallel consumer processing at our scale.
stories.task: We needed to resolve this before the system design was locked in, and both of us felt strongly about our approach.
stories.action: Instead of escalating, I suggested we each write up our approach with expected throughput numbers and review together. I showed that at 400K events/day, single partitions would bottleneck our consumers. He showed valid concerns about consumer group complexity. We combined both views — multiple partitions but with a managed consumer group strategy.
stories.result: The hybrid approach worked. We hit sub-200ms latency targets and the system scaled cleanly. The conflict made the design stronger than either of our original proposals.

stories.title: Code Review Disagreement at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, a senior engineer on my team left a blunt code review on my IoT pipeline implementation, saying my approach to handling MQTT reconnections was 'naive' and would fail under load.
stories.task: I felt the feedback was delivered harshly but I needed to figure out if they were technically right, and respond professionally either way.
stories.action: I took a day to research MQTT reconnection patterns, ran load tests on my implementation, and found they were partially correct — my exponential backoff logic had a bug under high-frequency disconnect events. I fixed the bug, added a test case for it, and replied to the review with both the fix and a note thanking them for catching it.
stories.result: The engineer respected that I engaged with the feedback technically rather than defensively. We ended up pairing on two more features together and the pipeline reduced downtime detection by 40% as planned.

stories.title: Open Source PR Conflict
stories.situation: While contributing to pgjdbc, another contributor submitted a PR that solved the same issue I was working on, but using a different approach. There was a brief back-and-forth in the comments about whose approach should be merged.
stories.task: I needed to either defend my approach or find a collaborative path forward — without making it personal in a public open source thread.
stories.action: I read their implementation carefully, acknowledged what was better about it, and pointed out one specific case my approach handled that theirs didn't. I proposed merging the best parts of both. The maintainer appreciated that I engaged constructively rather than protectively.
stories.result: My PR was merged with an element of their approach incorporated. Both contributors were credited. It reinforced for me that in collaborative environments, the best idea wins — not the loudest voice.

question: Tell me about a time you failed. How did you deal with the situation?

stories.title: MedStroke ML Accuracy
stories.situation: On MedStroke AI, I was responsible for the Random Forest model that analysed blood sample data to predict stroke risk. I set an internal target of 80% accuracy before our hospital pilot.
stories.task: When we reached the pilot date, the model was only at 45% accuracy — well below what I'd promised the team and the hospital contact.
stories.action: I was transparent with the team immediately rather than hiding the number. I did a root cause analysis — the training data was too small and imbalanced. I expanded the feature set, applied SMOTE for class balancing, and ran additional cross-validation. I also reframed the tool with the hospital as a 'decision support aid' rather than a diagnostic replacement, which was actually more appropriate clinically.
stories.result: The pilot went ahead and reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. The lower accuracy turned into a lesson about setting honest expectations and designing AI tools for clinical contexts — the doctors trusted a transparent tool more than a confident one.

stories.title: NanoSQL B-Tree Bug
stories.situation: Building NanoSQL, my SQLite clone in C, I implemented the B-tree and shipped it to a self-imposed deadline without fully testing node splitting under large datasets.
stories.task: Days later I discovered a corruption bug — pages were being overwritten during splits, destroying stored data silently.
stories.action: I rolled back the B-tree implementation, wrote a comprehensive test suite for split and rebalance operations first, then rebuilt the implementation test-first. It took an extra week but I caught three more edge cases I hadn't thought of initially.
stories.result: The final B-tree handled dynamic node splitting and rebalancing correctly under all test conditions. I learned to never skip tests on data structures that touch persistent storage — the cost of a bug there is data loss, not just wrong output.

stories.title: Missing Edge Cases at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, I submitted my first batch of integration tests for the IoT pipeline with confidence. A senior engineer's review found that I'd missed concurrent write scenarios — two sensors reporting simultaneously to the same topic.
stories.task: My tests had passed, but they weren't testing the right things. I had to acknowledge that and fix both the tests and potentially the implementation.
stories.action: I rewrote the test suite to include concurrent scenarios using JUnit's parallel execution support, found two real race conditions in the pipeline code, and patched them. I also adopted a checklist for concurrent systems going forward.
stories.result: The updated suite caught both race conditions before production. The pipeline shipped without any concurrency incidents. My manager noted the test quality improvement in my end-of-internship feedback.

question: Describe a time when you led a team. What was the outcome?

stories.title: NSBE Chapter Growth
stories.situation: When I became Assistant VP of NSBE at Grambling State, the chapter had low attendance and no corporate partnerships.
stories.task: My goal was to rebuild energy around the chapter, grow membership, and create real opportunities for CS students.
stories.action: I reorganized our events calendar around what students actually needed — resume workshops, technical mock interviews, and networking mixers. I personally reached out to tech companies for sponsorships, pitching the value of our talent pipeline. I delegated event leads to officers so no single person was overwhelmed.
stories.result: We grew to 300+ active members and secured $3K in company partnerships. Students who joined got access to opportunities they wouldn't have found otherwise, and the chapter became one of the more active technical communities on campus.

stories.title: MedStroke AI Project
stories.situation: MedStroke AI was a team project and I took the lead on the overall system architecture and the ML pipeline specifically.
stories.task: I needed to coordinate frontend, backend, and ML components while keeping the team aligned on the hospital pilot deadline.
stories.action: I set up weekly syncs, created a shared design doc so everyone understood how the pieces connected, and personally owned the highest-risk component — the Random Forest ML model. When we hit accuracy issues, I kept the team focused on what we could control: data quality, feature engineering, and the clinical framing of the tool.
stories.result: We shipped on time, piloted at a Louisiana hospital, and reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. The project became the centerpiece of my portfolio and demonstrated that I can lead both technically and operationally.

stories.title: Ericsson Dashboard Initiative
stories.situation: At Ericsson, nobody had been assigned to build visibility tooling for our distributed Kafka pipeline — engineers were debugging blind.
stories.task: I decided to take ownership of this gap and lead the effort to build a React dashboard for the team, even though it wasn't in my sprint.
stories.action: I scoped the dashboard myself, aligned with two other engineers on what data they needed most, and built it using React — visualizing distributed pipeline timelines in real time. I drove the entire delivery from design to deployment.
stories.result: The dashboard reduced debugging and resolution time from 60 minutes to 10 minutes for the 30-engineer team. My manager highlighted it as one of the most impactful contributions of the internship.

question: Tell me about a time you worked well under pressure.

stories.title: Sub-200ms Latency at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, two weeks before the Kafka microservice was due to launch, load testing showed our p99 latency was sitting at 380ms — nearly double our 200ms SLA.
stories.task: I had to find and fix the bottleneck fast without destabilising the rest of the pipeline.
stories.action: I profiled the consumer group rebalancing behaviour and discovered that our partition assignment strategy was causing excessive rebalances under load. I switched to a sticky partition assignor, tuned the session timeout and heartbeat interval, and re-ran load tests in a staging environment. I also stayed focused and avoided the temptation to make multiple changes at once — one variable at a time.
stories.result: Latency dropped to consistently under 180ms. We shipped on time. Working under that pressure sharpened my instinct for systematic debugging rather than panic-driven guessing.

stories.title: IoT Pipeline Outage at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, our IoT sensor pipeline went down in production during a peak monitoring window — sensors stopped reporting and the ops team had no visibility into device status.
stories.task: I was the engineer who had built the MQTT-to-PostgreSQL ingestion layer and was responsible for getting it back up quickly.
stories.action: I stayed calm, checked the MQTT broker logs first, found a broker certificate expiry that was silently dropping connections, renewed the cert, and validated the fix in staging before pushing to production. I documented the issue and added a certificate expiry monitoring alert to prevent recurrence.
stories.result: The pipeline was restored within 40 minutes. The incident led to a monitoring improvement that would catch cert expiries 30 days in advance going forward. My manager cited my composure under pressure in the post-mortem.

stories.title: React 19 Open Source Deadline
stories.situation: I had an open PR against facebook/react targeting a confirmed StrictMode regression in the React 19 reconciler. The React team announced a release candidate window, meaning PRs needed to be review-ready within days.
stories.task: I had to get my PR to a mergeable state — clean, well-tested, and explained — under real time pressure in a high-visibility codebase.
stories.action: I cut every non-essential change from the PR to keep it focused, wrote a clear reproduction case, added a regression test, and wrote a thorough PR description explaining the reconciler behaviour. I responded to reviewer comments within hours.
stories.result: The PR stayed active through the RC window. Contributing to production systems at Meta's scale under time pressure confirmed that I can perform when the stakes are real.

question: Provide an example of a time when you had to make a difficult decision.

stories.title: Event-Driven vs REST at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, I had to decide between two architectures for the pricing data system: a simpler REST-based approach my manager was comfortable with, or an event-driven Apache Kafka system I believed was the right choice at our scale.
stories.task: The Kafka approach was more complex to build and operate, but REST polling at 400K daily events would have introduced significant latency and load on upstream services.
stories.action: I built a small benchmark, documented the tradeoffs honestly — including the operational overhead of Kafka — and presented both options with data. I recommended Kafka but made clear it was a recommendation, not a demand. I also offered to own the extra complexity.
stories.result: We went with Kafka. The system processed 400K+ daily events across 6 topics at sub-200ms latency and replaced manual SQL workflows entirely. The harder decision produced the better outcome.

stories.title: Hospital Pilot Decision for MedStroke
stories.situation: With MedStroke AI, I had to decide whether to proceed with the Louisiana hospital pilot when our ML model was only at 45% accuracy — below my internal 80% target.
stories.task: Cancelling would have disappointed the hospital contact and delayed the project significantly. Proceeding meant piloting with a model that wasn't where I wanted it.
stories.action: I reframed the tool's clinical role from 'diagnostic replacement' to 'decision support aid,' consulted with the hospital contact on acceptable accuracy ranges for a first pilot, and was transparent about the model's limitations in all documentation.
stories.result: The pilot went ahead and reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. The hospital's feedback was positive specifically because we were honest about limitations. The difficult decision to go ahead — with transparency — was the right one.

stories.title: Dropping a Feature to Meet Deadline at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, two weeks before the IoT pipeline go-live, I realised we couldn't finish both the real-time alert feature and the historical replay feature with the quality bar I'd set.
stories.task: I had to decide which to cut — both were on the roadmap and stakeholders expected both.
stories.action: I mapped each feature to business impact, found that real-time alerts directly supported the 40% downtime-reduction goal while replay was a nice-to-have, and presented this analysis to my manager with a recommended phasing plan. I was clear about why — not to manage my workload, but to protect launch quality.
stories.result: We shipped real-time alerts on time. Downtime detection improved by 40%. The replay feature was scoped into the next sprint. Making the call early gave us time to do the remaining work well.

question: Describe a time when you went above and beyond the requirements for a project.

stories.title: Building the Ericsson Dashboard Unprompted
stories.situation: At Ericsson, my assigned scope was building the Kafka microservice and the event-driven pricing system. Nobody asked me to build any observability tooling.
stories.task: But I noticed engineers spending 60+ minutes per incident trying to trace events through the distributed pipeline with no visual tooling — just raw logs.
stories.action: On my own initiative, I scoped, designed, and built a React dashboard that visualised distributed pipeline timelines in real time. I aligned with two engineers on what data points mattered most, built it in React, and shipped it within the internship window.
stories.result: The dashboard cut debugging and resolution time from 60 minutes to 10 minutes for a 30-engineer team. My manager called it one of the most impactful contributions of the entire internship cohort — and it was never on my sprint board.

stories.title: 50+ Tests at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, the expectation for the IoT pipeline project was basic test coverage — maybe 15-20 unit tests to verify core flows.
stories.task: I believed that for a pipeline handling real sensor data, basic coverage wasn't enough. Silent failures in IoT systems cost real money.
stories.action: I wrote 50+ unit and integration tests using SpringBoot, JUnit, and Postman — covering concurrent writes, edge cases in the MQTT broker reconnection logic, and failure scenarios in the PostgreSQL ingestion layer. I went well beyond what was asked, on my own time.
stories.result: Backend reliability improved by 80% through catching issues before production. No pipeline incidents occurred during my remaining tenure. My manager pointed to the test suite as a model for the team going forward.

stories.title: Open Source Contributions
stories.situation: Contributing to open source wasn't part of any job description or academic requirement — I chose to do it entirely on my own.
stories.task: I wanted to work on production systems at scale that millions of developers and companies depend on — facebook/react, apache/kafka, pgjdbc, kubernetes-client/java.
stories.action: I found real bugs, studied the codebases deeply enough to propose fixes, wrote proper test cases and documentation, and engaged with maintainers through the review process. I got PRs merged into pgjdbc and kubernetes-client/java, and have an active PR against the React 19 reconciler targeting a confirmed StrictMode regression.
stories.result: I now have production contributions in systems powering Meta, LinkedIn, and JPMorgan. Nobody asked me to do this — I did it because I want to be the kind of engineer who shapes the tools we all use.

question: How do you handle a situation where you don't know the answer to a question?

stories.title: Learning F# at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, my team needed me to build an event-driven system using Apache Kafka and F# — a functional language I had never used before.
stories.task: Rather than pretend I knew it or ask to swap to a language I was comfortable in, I committed to learning F# properly.
stories.action: I was upfront with my manager that F# was new to me and gave a realistic estimate of the learning ramp. I spent the first week going through F# documentation, functional programming patterns, and async workflows. When I got stuck, I asked specific targeted questions rather than broad 'how do I do this' questions — which got me better answers faster.
stories.result: I delivered the event-driven system in F# on time. It aggregated 10K+ pricing records into RESTful endpoints and enabled real-time access to previously disconnected historical trade data. Being honest about what I didn't know upfront built trust and let me learn faster.

stories.title: MQTT Protocol at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, the IoT pipeline required expertise in the MQTT protocol — a lightweight messaging protocol for IoT devices I'd never worked with.
stories.task: I needed to design a reliable MQTT-to-PostgreSQL ingestion layer without previous experience in the protocol.
stories.action: I told my manager it was new territory, then spent two days on the MQTT spec and existing open source implementations before writing a line of code. When I had a specific question about QoS levels and message ordering guarantees, I asked a senior engineer — but came with a specific hypothesis first, not a blank question.
stories.result: The pipeline went live handling real sensor data with a 40% improvement in downtime detection. Admitting I didn't know something upfront and then doing the work to find out is faster than pretending and getting it wrong.

stories.title: React 19 Reconciler Internals
stories.situation: When I discovered a StrictMode regression in React 19, I needed to understand the reconciler internals — some of the most complex code in any JavaScript framework.
stories.task: I had no prior experience with React's internal reconciliation algorithm, and needed to go from zero to a credible fix proposal.
stories.action: I read the reconciler source, git-bisected the regression to a specific commit, wrote a minimal reproduction case, and posted a detailed issue before writing any fix. I was explicit in my PR about what I was confident in and what I wanted a maintainer to validate.
stories.result: The PR is active with maintainer engagement. Being transparent about the limits of my understanding — while still doing the hard work to find the root cause — is the approach that gets open source PRs taken seriously.

question: Describe a time you received tough or critical feedback.

stories.title: Kafka Consumer Design Review at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, after I submitted my Kafka consumer implementation for review, a senior engineer left detailed comments saying my consumer group configuration would cause excessive rebalancing under load — and that the design showed I didn't fully understand Kafka's consumer group protocol.
stories.task: The feedback was direct and a bit stinging, but I needed to figure out whether it was right and fix it if so.
stories.action: I took a full day to study Kafka consumer group internals and session timeout tuning before responding. The engineer was correct. I redesigned the consumer configuration using sticky partition assignment, updated the implementation, and wrote back with a clear explanation of what I'd changed and why — not just 'fixed it.'
stories.result: The updated implementation hit sub-200ms latency consistently under production load. The senior engineer later became one of my biggest advocates on the team. Tough feedback delivered well is a gift.

stories.title: pgjdbc PR Review
stories.situation: My first PR to pgjdbc came back with 14 review comments — some technical, some about code style, and one saying my approach to connection handling was 'reinventing what already exists in the library.'
stories.task: I had to absorb a large amount of critical feedback on a public platform, in a codebase maintained by engineers far more experienced than me.
stories.action: I went through every comment carefully, responded to each one individually, and spent two days studying the existing connection handling code the reviewer referenced. Three of the comments I agreed with and fixed. Two I pushed back on with data. I never responded defensively.
stories.result: The PR was merged after two revision rounds. The reviewer commented that my engagement with feedback was 'thorough and professional.' It's now a merged contribution to a library used by Java developers at JPMorgan and other financial institutions.

stories.title: MedStroke Team Code Review
stories.situation: During MedStroke development, a teammate reviewed my FastAPI endpoints and pointed out that my error handling was inconsistent — some endpoints returned 500 errors where I should have returned 422s with validation details.
stories.task: It was embarrassing because I'd considered myself the backend lead and this was a basic REST API design issue.
stories.action: I thanked them genuinely, audited every endpoint immediately, standardised the error response schema, and added a shared error handling middleware so the inconsistency couldn't recur. I also updated the API docs.
stories.result: The hospital pilot ran with zero API-related errors reported. More importantly, I stopped thinking of feedback as a judgment on my ability — it's just information that makes the product better.

question: Describe a time when you had to give someone difficult feedback. How did you handle it?

stories.title: NSBE Officer Feedback
stories.situation: As NSBE Assistant VP, one of our officers was consistently missing event prep deadlines, and other officers were quietly picking up the slack without saying anything.
stories.task: I needed to address it directly without damaging the relationship or demoralising someone who clearly cared about the chapter.
stories.action: I had a private one-on-one, not a group conversation. I started by acknowledging what they were doing well, then was specific and factual: 'The last three event prep deadlines were missed, and it created last-minute pressure for the team.' I asked them what was going on — and found out they were overwhelmed with coursework. We restructured their responsibilities together.
stories.result: Their reliability improved significantly over the next month. They thanked me later for addressing it privately and directly rather than letting it fester. The chapter ran more smoothly and their confidence actually grew from having clearer ownership.

stories.title: MedStroke ML Implementation
stories.situation: A teammate on MedStroke was responsible for data preprocessing and had been passing raw, unscaled features into the Random Forest model — which was hurting accuracy significantly.
stories.task: I needed to tell them their implementation was wrong, but they'd spent significant time on it and were proud of it.
stories.action: I framed it around the model performance data, not their effort: 'The accuracy is at 45% and I think I found why — let me show you what I'm seeing in the feature distributions.' I showed the unscaled values, explained why Random Forest is sensitive to it in this context, and asked if we could fix it together rather than handing it back to them as a failure.
stories.result: We fixed the preprocessing together in a pair session. The model improved. They felt involved in the solution rather than criticised. That approach — lead with data, collaborate on the fix — became my default for peer feedback.

stories.title: Code Review Feedback to a Peer at Ericsson
stories.situation: During code review at Ericsson, I noticed a fellow intern's Kafka producer implementation had a configuration issue — they were not setting proper acks settings, which would have caused silent data loss under network partitions.
stories.task: They were more senior among the interns and I was unsure how they'd receive feedback from me.
stories.action: I left a code review comment that was specific and explained the 'why': 'Setting acks=1 here means a broker crash between acknowledgment and replication could lose this message — for pricing events that's a real risk. Here's how to set acks=all with retries.' No judgment, just the technical consequence and the fix.
stories.result: They updated the configuration and thanked me in a follow-up comment. It opened up a broader conversation about Kafka reliability guarantees that the whole team benefited from. Good feedback is specific, factual, and explains consequences — not character.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to prioritize your tasks quickly.

stories.title: Production Bug vs Feature Work at Ericsson
stories.situation: Midway through building the Kafka microservice at Ericsson, a production issue was flagged in an earlier component I'd worked on — pricing data was arriving out of order in downstream consumers.
stories.task: I had active sprint commitments on the new microservice but a live production issue needed immediate attention.
stories.action: I immediately communicated to my manager that I was switching context to the production issue. I triaged it first to confirm severity — it was affecting live reporting — then paused all non-critical feature work. I fixed the consumer offset management issue, tested in staging, deployed, and only returned to the microservice once the fix was confirmed stable.
stories.result: The production issue was resolved within the day. I then reprioritised the remaining microservice tasks to recover the schedule. My manager appreciated the transparency and decisiveness — switching context without communicating would have been worse.

stories.title: NSBE + Internship + School Simultaneously
stories.situation: During my first semester as NSBE Assistant VP, I was simultaneously handling internship applications, coursework for four CS classes, and our biggest NSBE event of the semester.
stories.task: Everything felt urgent and I had to decide what actually was.
stories.action: I applied the urgency/importance matrix. NSBE event logistics had a hard date — that was time-boxed and delegated where possible. Coursework deadlines were fixed — I blocked study time daily and didn't let it slip. Internship applications I batched into weekend sessions. I said no to two extra NSBE commitments that week that weren't load-bearing.
stories.result: The NSBE event ran successfully, I finished the semester with my 3.93 GPA intact, and I landed my Ericsson offer. Prioritising well doesn't mean doing everything — it means knowing what not to do.

stories.title: Simultaneous Noon Deadlines
stories.situation: At Noon, during the final two weeks of the IoT pipeline project, a new bug was discovered in the PostgreSQL schema that affected data integrity, on the same day my test suite was due for review.
stories.task: Both tasks were high priority. The bug was a data integrity issue — silent failures — and the test suite review had a fixed team meeting it was anchored to.
stories.action: I assessed which had irreversible consequences: the data integrity bug, if left unfixed, would corrupt records silently. I triaged the schema bug first, patched and validated it in a few hours, then delivered the test suite to review with a brief explanation of why it was coming in late. I communicated the tradeoff before the review rather than after.
stories.result: Both were delivered the same day. My manager said the triage judgment was exactly right — the schema bug was the more dangerous risk.

question: Describe a time when you anticipated potential problems and developed preventive measures.

stories.title: Load Testing Before Ericsson Launch
stories.situation: Before launching the Kafka microservice at Ericsson to process 400K+ daily events, I anticipated that our consumer throughput had never been tested at production-equivalent volumes.
stories.task: Rather than find out at launch, I wanted to proactively stress-test the system and identify bottlenecks in a controlled environment.
stories.action: I set up a load testing environment that replayed a synthetic event stream at 2x our expected daily volume over a compressed time window. I monitored consumer lag, partition rebalancing frequency, and end-to-end latency. I found a rebalancing issue that appeared only under sustained load — invisible in normal testing.
stories.result: I fixed the partition assignor configuration before production launch. The system hit sub-200ms latency from day one with no consumer lag incidents. Anticipating that load scenario saved a potential production crisis.

stories.title: MQTT Reconnection Edge Cases at Noon
stories.situation: When building the IoT pipeline at Noon, I knew from research that MQTT brokers have known edge cases around reconnection under poor network conditions — something common in IoT environments.
stories.task: The standard implementation wouldn't handle this — I needed to build resilience in before we went live with real sensors.
stories.action: I implemented exponential backoff for reconnection attempts, added circuit breaker logic so the pipeline wouldn't hammer a recovering broker, and wrote integration tests that simulated network partitions by forcibly disconnecting the test broker mid-stream.
stories.result: During UAT, we deliberately killed the broker connection and the pipeline recovered gracefully with zero data loss. The preventive design directly contributed to the 40% reduction in downtime detection — the system didn't create new downtime while recovering from events.

stories.title: MedStroke False Positive Risk
stories.situation: Before piloting MedStroke AI at the hospital, I anticipated that a model producing false negatives (missing real stroke risk) would be far more dangerous than false positives in a clinical context.
stories.task: I needed to configure the model threshold to reflect clinical priorities — not just maximise overall accuracy.
stories.action: I tuned the Random Forest classification threshold to bias toward sensitivity over specificity, added a clear confidence score output so doctors could weight the tool appropriately, and flagged high-uncertainty predictions explicitly in the UI.
stories.result: The hospital doctors reported that the confidence display and threshold tuning made the tool feel clinically trustworthy. The pilot reduced time-to-treatment by 35% and the medical staff adopted it rather than ignoring it — which is the real metric for a clinical tool.

question: Describe a situation where you had to deal with a difficult customer or stakeholder.

stories.title: Hospital Doctors Skeptical of MedStroke AI
stories.situation: During the MedStroke AI hospital pilot in Louisiana, the neurologists were initially skeptical of the AI tool — they felt it was oversimplifying a nuanced clinical judgment and worried it would undermine their authority.
stories.task: I needed to address their concerns honestly without overpromising what the model could do.
stories.action: I met with them in person, listened to every concern without being defensive, and adjusted how I described the tool — not as a diagnostic replacement but as a pattern-recognition aid they could choose to consult or ignore. I also added a confidence score display and made the model's reasoning more visible so they could interrogate it.
stories.result: The doctors' engagement shifted from resistance to active curiosity. The pilot reduced time-to-treatment by 35% because doctors adopted the tool on their own terms. The lesson: technical capability alone doesn't earn stakeholder trust — transparency and listening do.

stories.title: Corporate Sponsorship for NSBE
stories.situation: As NSBE Assistant VP, I approached several tech companies for sponsorships. One company's HR contact was initially dismissive — they'd had poor engagement with student orgs in the past and didn't see the ROI.
stories.task: I needed to change their perception and make the case for partnership without being pushy.
stories.action: I came back with a concrete proposal: specific events where their engineers could interact with our 300+ members, data on our members' GPA distribution and job placement goals, and a cost-per-engagement number that compared favourably to career fair costs. I made it easy for them to say yes.
stories.result: They signed on and we secured $3K in partnerships total across companies. The contact later told me the proposal stood out because it was specific and ROI-driven rather than generic. That taught me that difficult stakeholders usually have a legitimate reason for their resistance — your job is to understand it.

stories.title: Shifting Requirements at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, partway through the IoT pipeline build, the client's requirements shifted — they now wanted historical data replay in addition to real-time ingestion, which hadn't been scoped.
stories.task: I needed to manage expectations with both the client and my manager without either overpromising or damaging the relationship.
stories.action: I documented exactly what the new feature would require in terms of schema changes and additional development time, presented two options — full scope with a revised timeline or phased delivery with real-time first — and recommended the phased approach clearly. I let the client choose with full information.
stories.result: They chose phased delivery. Real-time went live on schedule with 40% downtime reduction. The client appreciated being given real options rather than a surprise delay. Managing difficult stakeholders is about giving them honest choices, not just good news.

question: Tell me about a time when you missed a deadline. What happened, and how did you handle it?

stories.title: NanoSQL B-Tree Overrun
stories.situation: I set a personal deadline to complete NanoSQL — my SQLite clone in C — within six weeks. The B-tree implementation took three additional weeks beyond my estimate.
stories.task: The deadline was self-imposed, but I treated it seriously as a commitment to myself and used it as a learning exercise in estimation.
stories.action: When I realised I was going to miss it, I did a root cause analysis rather than just pushing harder. I'd underestimated the complexity of page allocation and node rebalancing under concurrent access. I revised my estimate based on what was actually left, communicated it to myself in writing, and broke the remaining work into daily checkpoints.
stories.result: The final B-tree was correct, well-tested, and documented. The miss taught me that estimation requires decomposing work to the level of individual operations, not just features. I now break tasks into smaller units before estimating anything.

stories.title: MedStroke ML Iteration Delay
stories.situation: The original MedStroke timeline had the ML model ready for integration two weeks before the hospital pilot. I missed that internal deadline by five days due to underestimating data preprocessing complexity.
stories.task: The team needed the model to integrate with the FastAPI backend and I was the blocker.
stories.action: The moment I knew I'd miss it, I told the team immediately — not the day it was due. I gave them an honest revised estimate, offered to work in parallel with their integration once I had a working but imperfect model, and delivered a checkpoint version for them to build against while I continued improving it.
stories.result: Integration started on schedule using the checkpoint model. The final model was delivered two days after the original deadline — but because I communicated early, it cost the project zero days. Early transparency on a slip is almost as good as not slipping.

stories.title: Noon Pipeline Integration Delay
stories.situation: At Noon, the IoT pipeline integration with PostgreSQL hit unexpected schema compatibility issues that delayed delivery by three days relative to the sprint commitment.
stories.task: The team was counting on the pipeline being ready to feed the notification system I was handing off to.
stories.action: I flagged the issue two days before the deadline — not the day of. I explained exactly what the blocker was, proposed a workaround that would let the downstream team begin their work with stubbed data, and took full ownership of the delay rather than attributing it to the schema issue.
stories.result: The downstream team used the stub data and stayed unblocked. The real integration landed three days late but caused zero cascading delays. Missing a deadline gracefully means communicating early and protecting the people depending on you.

question: Describe a time when your workload was heavy and how you handled it.

stories.title: Ericsson + Coursework Simultaneously
stories.situation: During my Ericsson apprenticeship I was simultaneously enrolled in courses and managing three concurrent deliverables: the Kafka microservice, the event-driven pricing system, and the React dashboard.
stories.task: All three had overlapping timelines and I was the sole engineer on each.
stories.action: I time-blocked my week rigidly — deep engineering work in mornings, meetings and reviews in afternoons, coursework in evenings. I identified which of the three projects had the hardest external dependencies and sequenced around those. I also dropped non-essential activities for the duration.
stories.result: All three shipped on time. The pipeline hit sub-200ms latency, the pricing system went live, and the dashboard cut debugging time from 60 to 10 minutes. Heavy workloads are manageable when you sequence deliberately instead of reacting.

stories.title: NSBE Leadership + School + Applications
stories.situation: In the semester I became NSBE Assistant VP I was also carrying 18 credit hours and applying to summer internships.
stories.task: Each of those three things was a full commitment on its own. I had to handle all three without any of them slipping.
stories.action: I treated my week like a product with limited capacity. I set fixed slots for NSBE work — Sunday planning, Tuesday events — and protected study blocks as non-negotiable. I batched internship application writing into one Saturday morning per week. I said no to commitments that didn't map to any of those three priorities.
stories.result: We grew NSBE to 300+ members that semester, I maintained a 3.93 GPA, and I landed the Ericsson offer. The key was treating my time like a constrained resource, not a flexible one.

stories.title: MedStroke + Open Source + Academics
stories.situation: During a particularly intense stretch I was developing MedStroke AI toward a hospital pilot deadline, actively reviewing PRs on pgjdbc and kubernetes-client/java, and finishing a Data Structures & Algorithms course.
stories.task: Each had real external deadlines — the hospital, the open source release windows, and my professor.
stories.action: I used a simple rule: external deadlines trump internal ones. Hospital pilot prep came first. Open source reviews I batched into 30-minute windows between study sessions. I used the algorithms coursework to deepen my understanding of the B-tree work I was doing in NanoSQL — learning that served multiple goals simultaneously.
stories.result: The hospital pilot launched on schedule. Two PRs merged. I got an A in the course. Finding tasks that serve multiple goals is one of the best ways to handle a heavy workload.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to deal with a significant change at work. How did you adapt?

stories.title: Learning F# Mid-Project at Ericsson
stories.situation: When I joined Ericsson's pricing data project, I found out the system I was building needed to be written in F# — a functional language I had never used.
stories.task: I had to get productive in F# fast while delivering a production system, not a toy project.
stories.action: I spent the first week fully in learning mode: F# documentation, async workflows, immutable data patterns. I asked senior engineers specific questions — not 'how does F# work' but 'how does F# handle this specific Kafka consumer pattern.' I treated functional programming as a mindset shift, not just syntax.
stories.result: I built the event-driven system in F# and it aggregated 10K+ pricing records into RESTful endpoints enabling real-time access to previously disconnected historical data. The change forced me to grow in a direction I wouldn't have chosen — and it made me a stronger engineer.

stories.title: React 19 Reconciler Changes
stories.situation: When React 19 shipped major changes to the reconciler, code paths I was working with in my open source contribution suddenly behaved differently than the React 18 documentation described.
stories.task: I had to adapt my mental model of React's rendering behaviour and update my PR accordingly.
stories.action: I read the React 19 migration guide, the relevant RFC, and then the actual reconciler source code. I updated my reproduction case to confirm the regression still existed in the new version, rewrote my PR description to reference the React 19 behaviour explicitly, and added a new test for the changed code path.
stories.result: The PR stayed relevant through the React 19 release. Adapting to change in a fast-moving codebase requires going to primary sources — docs, RFCs, and source code — not waiting for tutorials.

stories.title: Batch to Real-Time at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, the initial IoT data pipeline design used batch ingestion — processing sensor data every 5 minutes. Mid-project, requirements changed to real-time streaming.
stories.task: I had to pivot the architecture while preserving the work already done and without blowing the timeline.
stories.action: I assessed what was reusable — the PostgreSQL schema and the data transformation logic — and what needed to change: the ingestion layer needed to shift from scheduled pulls to MQTT streaming. I restructured the ingestion module around Kafka Connect and MQTT without touching the transformation layer.
stories.result: The real-time pipeline shipped on schedule. The architectural separation between ingestion and transformation made the pivot possible without a rewrite. Planning for change means keeping your layers clean.

question: Describe a situation where you saw a problem and took the initiative to correct it rather than waiting for someone else.

stories.title: Ericsson React Dashboard
stories.situation: At Ericsson, engineers on the team were spending 60+ minutes per incident tracing events through the distributed Kafka pipeline using raw logs with no visual tooling.
stories.task: Nobody had been assigned to fix this — it was background pain everyone accepted. I decided not to.
stories.action: I proactively scoped a React dashboard, aligned with two engineers on the most useful data points, built it on top of our existing pipeline metrics, and shipped it within the internship window without it ever appearing on a sprint board.
stories.result: Debugging and resolution time dropped from 60 minutes to 10 minutes for a 30-engineer team. My manager called it one of the most impactful contributions of the internship. The initiative cost me evenings — it returned hours to thirty engineers every week.

stories.title: Automated Tests at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, I noticed the IoT pipeline had no automated test suite — just manual testing before each deployment.
stories.task: Manual testing at this cadence was unsustainable and risky for a pipeline handling live sensor data. I decided to fix it.
stories.action: I proposed a testing framework to my manager, got a quick green light, and built 50+ unit and integration tests using SpringBoot, JUnit, and Postman — covering normal flows, edge cases, and failure scenarios the team hadn't been testing at all.
stories.result: Backend reliability improved by 80%. The team adopted the test suite as standard and it became the baseline for subsequent development. I took initiative not because it was glamorous but because the risk of not doing it was obvious.

stories.title: NSBE Partnership Drive
stories.situation: When I became NSBE Assistant VP, the chapter had no corporate partnerships and was entirely self-funded through small dues.
stories.task: Funding was limiting what we could offer members. Nobody had been tasked with fixing this — it was just accepted.
stories.action: I built a sponsorship deck, identified companies whose engineering culture aligned with our membership's interests, and cold-contacted 12 companies over two months. I personalised each pitch around their specific campus recruiting goals and our member demographics.
stories.result: We secured $3K in partnerships with multiple companies. That funding let us run events that wouldn't have been possible otherwise. Taking initiative on the unglamorous work — sales, outreach, follow-ups — is often what actually moves an organisation forward.

question: Describe a time when there was a conflict within your team. How did you help resolve it? Did you do anything to prevent it in future?

stories.title: Architecture Conflict on MedStroke
stories.situation: On MedStroke, two teammates disagreed sharply on the ML model selection — one wanted a neural network for higher potential accuracy, the other wanted Random Forest for interpretability in a clinical context.
stories.task: The disagreement was stalling the project and both were right in different ways.
stories.action: I facilitated a structured comparison rather than letting it stay abstract. I asked each person to document their approach with concrete tradeoffs relevant to our specific constraints: dataset size, interpretability requirement for doctors, and our timeline. Once it was written down, the clinical interpretability requirement made the decision clear.
stories.result: We chose Random Forest. The neural network advocate accepted the decision because they saw the clinical reasoning applied to our specific context. To prevent future conflicts, I proposed we document architectural decisions with explicit context and constraints so debates are grounded in facts, not preferences.

stories.title: Kafka vs REST at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, two engineers on the wider team had an ongoing low-level conflict about whether to use Kafka or REST for a new internal service — it was spilling into planning meetings and creating friction.
stories.task: As someone who'd just built both types of systems, I was asked to help resolve it.
stories.action: I suggested a time-boxed spike: each approach gets two days to prototype the same feature with the same performance constraints. Both engineers built their version. The data from the spike made the decision obvious without anyone needing to 'win' the argument.
stories.result: The conflict dissolved because the answer came from evidence, not opinion. I proposed adopting the spike approach as a default for future architecture disagreements — make the decision empirical, not political.

stories.title: Test Priority Conflict at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, two engineers disagreed on whether to prioritise writing tests or pushing new features before the pipeline launch. The disagreement was creating tension in standups.
stories.task: I saw the conflict was really about risk tolerance, not tests vs features — both were legitimate concerns.
stories.action: I brought both engineers together and reframed the question: 'What's the minimum test coverage that makes us comfortable shipping?' That shifted the conversation from opposition to collaboration. We defined a coverage threshold together and agreed to gates — certain flows needed tests before the feature could merge.
stories.result: The conflict ended because we turned it into a shared policy both could live with. In the future, having a pre-agreed merge policy meant similar conflicts didn't recur.

question: Describe a time when you went out of your comfort zone. Why did you do it? What lessons did you learn?

stories.title: Contributing to facebook/react
stories.situation: Contributing to facebook/react meant submitting code that would run in production for millions of developers worldwide — a very different stakes level from any project I'd worked on before.
stories.task: I'd never worked on a codebase at that scale or complexity. The React 19 reconciler is some of the most sophisticated JavaScript ever written.
stories.action: I went in anyway. I spent weeks reading the reconciler source before writing a line, found a genuine StrictMode regression, wrote a minimal reproduction case, submitted a detailed issue, and opened a PR with a test. I was explicit about what I was confident in and invited maintainer review.
stories.result: The PR is active with maintainer engagement. Going out of my comfort zone into the most complex JS codebase in existence taught me that the gap between 'too scary to touch' and 'I can contribute here' is mostly just time spent reading source code.

stories.title: NSBE Public Leadership
stories.situation: Before becoming NSBE Assistant VP, I was comfortable in technical environments but had never led a large community or done public speaking regularly.
stories.task: The role required running chapter meetings for 300+ members, speaking to corporate sponsors, and representing student interests to university administration.
stories.action: I pushed myself to take every speaking opportunity, prepared obsessively for the first few, and actively sought feedback after each event. I also studied how the most effective NSBE leaders at national conferences communicated — specific, story-driven, energetic.
stories.result: By the end of the first semester I was comfortable leading meetings, negotiating with sponsors, and speaking at events. I secured $3K in partnerships partly on the strength of in-person pitches. Going out of your comfort zone in communication skills compounds — every opportunity makes the next one easier.

stories.title: Building in Go and C from Scratch
stories.situation: Go and C were outside my primary tech stack when I decided to build Go Container and NanoSQL.
stories.task: I chose those languages deliberately — Go for its systems concurrency model, C for direct memory management — because I wanted to understand what higher-level languages abstract away.
stories.action: I started each project by reading language specs and existing implementations before writing code. For Go Container I read the Linux namespaces and cgroups kernel documentation. For NanoSQL I read the SQLite design document before touching a B-tree.
stories.result: I built a container runtime with process isolation, namespace separation, and resource controls — and a database engine with persistent B-tree storage. The discomfort of working in unfamiliar languages at the systems level gave me a depth of understanding I couldn't have gotten any other way.

question: Describe a time when you delivered a project under a tight deadline.

stories.title: Ericsson Kafka Microservice
stories.situation: At Ericsson, the Kafka microservice processing 400K+ daily events had a hard launch deadline tied to a business reporting cycle — it couldn't slip.
stories.task: I had to deliver a production-quality distributed system with sub-200ms latency SLA in a fixed window, while also learning the codebase and the Ericsson deployment environment.
stories.action: I front-loaded the riskiest work — the Kafka consumer group configuration and latency tuning — so I had maximum time to fix problems. I ran load tests early rather than late, found the rebalancing issue two weeks before launch, and fixed it with time to spare. I cut scope on nice-to-haves without being asked.
stories.result: Launched on time, hitting sub-200ms latency from day one, replacing manual SQL workflows across the team. Tight deadlines reward front-loading risk.

stories.title: MedStroke Hospital Pilot
stories.situation: The Louisiana hospital had a fixed date for the MedStroke AI pilot — a date set before the ML model was even close to ready.
stories.task: I had to deliver a working stroke diagnosis system with role-based access, NIHSS scoring, and automated tPA eligibility within a non-negotiable window.
stories.action: I scoped ruthlessly — everything that wasn't directly patient-facing was deferred. I set daily checkpoints rather than weekly, so slippage was caught in hours not days. I delivered a checkpoint model for integration while still improving it in parallel.
stories.result: The pilot launched on time and reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. Daily checkpoints and ruthless scoping are the two most effective tools for tight deadline delivery.

stories.title: Noon IoT Pipeline
stories.situation: At Noon, the IoT pipeline had to go live before a client demo — and the client had already announced the demo date before we started building.
stories.task: Live sensor data had to flow through MQTT into PostgreSQL and appear in a monitoring dashboard in time for the demo.
stories.action: I separated the integration concerns clearly — MQTT ingestion, transformation, and PostgreSQL storage were built as independent, testable units. This let me parallelize work with another engineer without coupling. I ran end-to-end smoke tests daily in the final two weeks rather than waiting for a full integration test at the end.
stories.result: The pipeline was live for the client demo. Daily smoke tests meant we caught integration issues 48 hours before they would have been discovered otherwise. Parallel work and early integration testing are the fastest path through a tight deadline.

question: Describe a time when you took a big risk and it failed.

stories.title: MedStroke ML Accuracy Miss
stories.situation: For MedStroke AI, I committed to an 80% accuracy target for the Random Forest stroke risk model before I had fully assessed the dataset quality and size.
stories.task: I was confident in the architecture but hadn't pressure-tested the assumption that our dataset was sufficient for that accuracy level.
stories.action: When the model plateaued at 45%, I ran a root cause: the dataset was too small and class-imbalanced. I tried SMOTE balancing, feature engineering, and hyperparameter tuning. I got to 45% — meaningfully better than a random classifier, but not 80%.
stories.result: I missed the accuracy target I'd committed to. I was transparent with the team, reframed the tool's clinical role as decision support rather than diagnosis, and the hospital pilot still ran successfully. The lesson: never commit to an accuracy target before validating your dataset — that's where confidence should come from.

stories.title: NanoSQL Custom Memory Allocator
stories.situation: In NanoSQL, I decided to implement a custom memory allocator for page management rather than using standard malloc — believing it would give me tighter control over fragmentation.
stories.task: This was ambitious and unnecessary for the project's stated goals, but I wanted to try it.
stories.action: I spent a week building the allocator. It worked in simple cases but introduced subtle bugs when pages were freed and reallocated under mixed read/write workloads — corruption that was nearly impossible to reproduce reliably.
stories.result: I scrapped the custom allocator and used standard C memory management. The B-tree became stable and the project shipped. The risk failed because I took on unnecessary complexity for a learning goal — which is fine in a personal project, but taught me to separate learning experiments from production code paths.

stories.title: Over-Scoping the Ericsson Dashboard
stories.situation: When I built the React dashboard at Ericsson, I initially tried to include predictive analytics — forecasting consumer lag before it became critical.
stories.task: I allocated time to the prediction feature and it pulled me away from the core visualisation work.
stories.action: The prediction model required historical baseline data we hadn't collected yet. I spent three days on it before accepting it wasn't going to be ready. I cut the feature entirely and refocused on the core timeline visualisation.
stories.result: The core dashboard shipped and cut debugging time from 60 to 10 minutes. The prediction feature would have added marginal value but nearly sank the timeline. The risk of over-scoping taught me: ship the thing that solves the core problem first.

question: How would you design or test a product to make sure it's diverse and inclusive to all users?

stories.title: MedStroke Designed for Non-Technical Doctors
stories.situation: MedStroke AI was going to be used by doctors and nurses with varying levels of technology comfort — from residents who used tablets daily to senior neurologists who preferred paper notes.
stories.task: I needed to design an interface that didn't assume technical fluency but also didn't condescend to clinically sophisticated users.
stories.action: I interviewed two nurses and one doctor before designing the UI. I kept the primary workflow to three steps, added clear confidence scores and uncertainty indicators, and made the AI output secondary to the NIHSS scoring tool doctors already trusted. I tested with a non-technical staff member and iterated until they could use it without instructions.
stories.result: The hospital staff adopted the tool during the pilot without a formal training session. Inclusive design isn't about adding features — it's about removing barriers through early user research.

stories.title: Ericsson Dashboard for a 30-Engineer Team
stories.situation: The React dashboard I built at Ericsson needed to serve engineers at every seniority level — from junior devs unfamiliar with Kafka internals to architects who wanted raw partition metrics.
stories.task: A single view couldn't serve both audiences. I needed to design progressive disclosure.
stories.action: I built a default view showing high-level pipeline health — consumer lag, throughput, error rates — with drill-down panels for partition-level metrics. New engineers could read the top-level view immediately; senior engineers could drill down. I tested the default view with two junior engineers before shipping.
stories.result: The dashboard was adopted across the full team. Both senior and junior engineers used it in incident resolution. Progressive disclosure is the core pattern for building tools that work for diverse users.

stories.title: NSBE Events for 300+ Diverse Members
stories.situation: Our NSBE chapter had members ranging from first-year students who'd never seen a technical interview to seniors with multiple internships — plus students from non-CS majors interested in technology careers.
stories.task: Events had to be valuable across that entire range without talking down to advanced members or overwhelming beginners.
stories.action: I structured events in tiers — 'intro' sessions for fundamentals and 'advanced' workshops for students who already had internship experience. I surveyed members after each event and used the feedback to adjust. For networking events I briefed corporate sponsors to bring engineers at different levels.
stories.result: Attendance grew to 300+ active members with strong retention across all cohorts. Diversity-aware programming isn't a nice-to-have — it's the only way to build a community that actually sticks.

question: Describe a time you had to explain a complex technical concept to someone non-technical.

stories.title: Explaining Kafka to Ericsson Stakeholders
stories.situation: At Ericsson, I had to present the Kafka-based pricing system to business stakeholders who needed to understand why we chose event streaming over a database-based approach.
stories.task: These were senior business people — not engineers. They needed to understand the value proposition, not the implementation.
stories.action: I used an analogy: instead of the system checking the price list every few minutes like a store clerk asking 'anything changed?', Kafka was like having a manager shout every price change the instant it happened — 400,000 times a day. I focused on what it meant for them: real-time pricing data that used to take hours of manual SQL work now appeared instantly.
stories.result: Stakeholders understood and approved the approach. One said it was the first time a technical presentation had made them feel confident rather than confused. Analogies that tie to business outcomes are the fastest path to non-technical understanding.

stories.title: Explaining ML to Hospital Doctors
stories.situation: When piloting MedStroke AI, I needed to explain to neurologists how the Random Forest model made predictions — doctors are clinically sophisticated but not ML practitioners.
stories.task: I needed them to understand enough to trust and appropriately use the tool — not to distrust it blindly or over-rely on it.
stories.action: I explained it as pattern recognition over thousands of past cases: 'The model has seen 10,000 patients with these blood values, and 73% of them had stroke events within 24 hours. It's showing you the historical pattern, not making a diagnosis.' I then showed the confidence score and explained what low confidence meant in clinical terms.
stories.result: The doctors engaged with the tool critically and constructively — exactly what I wanted. They flagged two cases where their clinical judgment overrode the model, which was correct behaviour. The explanation built appropriate trust, not blind faith.

stories.title: Explaining Containers to NSBE Members
stories.situation: At an NSBE workshop I presented Go Container, my container runtime project. Most attendees were first and second year CS students unfamiliar with Linux internals.
stories.task: I needed to explain Linux namespaces, cgroups, and chroot in a way that made people excited rather than overwhelmed.
stories.action: I used the analogy of a rented apartment: a container is like each tenant getting their own front door, mailbox, and utilities meter — they share the building (the kernel) but can't see into each other's apartments or use more than their allocated electricity (cgroups). I live-coded a simple namespace isolation demo.
stories.result: Three students came up after to ask how to start building systems projects. One joined a Linux kernel study group I mentioned. Making complex technical ideas vivid and concrete is how you get people to actually go build things.

question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a colleague. How did you handle the situation?

stories.title: Kafka Partitioning Strategy at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, a colleague wanted single partitions per Kafka topic for simplicity. I believed we needed multiple partitions to support parallel consumption at 400K daily events.
stories.task: We disagreed technically and needed to resolve it before the architecture was locked.
stories.action: I wrote up a throughput analysis showing consumer lag projections at our event volume with single vs multiple partitions. I presented it as information, not a verdict — and acknowledged their concern about consumer group complexity. We discussed the tradeoffs in a one-on-one before the team meeting.
stories.result: We agreed on multiple partitions with managed consumer group assignment. The system hit sub-200ms latency. The disagreement improved the design. Bringing data to a disagreement moves it from opinion to engineering.

stories.title: Integration vs Mock Testing at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, a colleague thought integration tests were overkill for the IoT pipeline — unit tests with mocked MQTT brokers were sufficient in their view.
stories.task: I disagreed because MQTT reconnection behaviour under real broker failures was impossible to test with mocks, and that was our highest-risk failure mode.
stories.action: I didn't argue in the abstract. I wrote one integration test that used a real test broker, simulated a disconnect, and demonstrated a failure mode that the mocked tests missed. I showed them the test result and let it speak.
stories.result: They agreed integration tests were worth it for the reconnection layer. We split the test suite — unit tests for transformation logic, integration tests for broker interaction. The empirical approach resolved the disagreement faster than debating principles.

stories.title: PostgreSQL vs MongoDB for MedStroke
stories.situation: On MedStroke, a teammate wanted to use MongoDB for patient data storage. I believed PostgreSQL was the right choice given the relational nature of NIHSS scoring, tPA eligibility, and patient history.
stories.task: We had a genuine technical disagreement and needed to pick one before building the data layer.
stories.action: I drew out the data model on paper — patient records, scoring events, eligibility flags — and showed how every query pattern we needed was a join. Then I asked them to describe a query that MongoDB would handle better. They couldn't find one that applied to our specific use case.
stories.result: We used PostgreSQL. The data model was clean and all queries were straightforward. I've since used this approach regularly: when disagreeing on a technology choice, draw the data model and query patterns first — the right answer often becomes obvious.

question: Give an example of a time you had to collaborate effectively with a team from a different department.

stories.title: MedStroke with Medical Staff
stories.situation: Building MedStroke AI required deep collaboration with the medical staff at the Louisiana hospital — neurologists and nurses who had no software engineering background and were skeptical of AI tools.
stories.task: I needed clinical input to design the NIHSS scoring interface and tPA eligibility logic correctly, but communicating across the technical-clinical divide was genuinely hard.
stories.action: I started by listening rather than presenting. I shadowed a clinician through one patient assessment to understand their actual workflow. Then I translated every technical question into clinical terms: not 'what data fields do you need' but 'what information do you look at first when a stroke patient comes in?' I created paper mockups before building anything and validated with the medical team before writing code.
stories.result: The tool fit into the clinical workflow naturally because it was built around that workflow. Time-to-treatment reduced by 35%. Cross-functional collaboration works when the technical team does the work of translation — not the other way around.

stories.title: NSBE with University Administration
stories.situation: To secure room bookings, co-sponsorship from the university, and access to career fair infrastructure, NSBE needed to collaborate with university administration — a department with very different incentives and communication styles.
stories.task: I needed to negotiate partnerships and access that served our members without creating friction with admin processes.
stories.action: I learned the university's processes before making requests. I brought documentation, showed how our events aligned with the university's diversity and retention goals, and always gave admins enough lead time. I treated it as a partnership, not a transaction — checking in after events with attendance numbers they could use in their own reporting.
stories.result: We built a reliable working relationship with administration that made future events smoother to organise. They became proactive partners rather than gatekeepers. The key insight: administrative departments are easier to work with when you understand their constraints and goals.

stories.title: Ericsson Cross-Functional Pipeline Work
stories.situation: At Ericsson, the Kafka pipeline I was building needed to integrate with an infrastructure team managing the Kubernetes deployment environment — a team I had no direct relationship with.
stories.task: I needed configuration support, environment access, and their buy-in on our deployment approach — without any formal reporting line.
stories.action: I requested a short sync early rather than late, came prepared with specific questions and a clear diagram of what I needed, and framed every request around reducing their work — 'here's the config I need, here's the format it needs to be in, and here's what I'll own vs what I need from you.' I also documented everything so they didn't need to chase me for context.
stories.result: The infrastructure team flagged our Kubernetes resource limits were too low before deployment — something I'd have discovered in production. That early collaboration caught a real problem. Cross-team work is easier when you make it easy for the other team.

question: Tell me about a complex technical project you've worked on.

stories.title: Ericsson Kafka Microservice at Scale
stories.situation: At Ericsson I built a Kafka-streaming microservice deployed on Kubernetes that processed 400K+ daily events across 6 topics, replacing a system of manual SQL workflows.
stories.task: The system needed sub-200ms end-to-end latency, fault tolerance across broker failures, and clean integration with upstream REST APIs and downstream reporting consumers.
stories.action: I designed the consumer group topology to balance partition load, used sticky partition assignment to reduce rebalancing under load, implemented idempotent producers to handle retries without duplicate events, and wrote a load test suite that simulated production volume before launch. I also built a React dashboard to make the pipeline observable.
stories.result: The system processed 400K+ daily events at sub-200ms latency, eliminated manual SQL workflows, and the dashboard cut incident resolution time from 60 to 10 minutes. It's the most production-impactful system I've built.

stories.title: MedStroke AI — Full-Stack Clinical Tool
stories.situation: MedStroke AI was a full-stack stroke diagnosis system with real-time patient monitoring, role-based access control, NIHSS scoring, automated tPA eligibility, and a Random Forest ML model — built for a hospital pilot.
stories.task: I was responsible for the ML pipeline, the FastAPI backend, and the overall system architecture.
stories.action: I designed the ML pipeline around a Random Forest model trained on blood sample data, implemented the NIHSS scoring logic against clinical guidelines, and built role-based access so neurologists and nurses had appropriate data visibility. I ran the hospital pilot iteratively — shipping, getting feedback, and shipping again within the pilot window.
stories.result: The system reduced time-to-treatment by 35% at the Louisiana hospital pilot. Building a healthcare AI system taught me that technical correctness and clinical trustworthiness are two different bars — you need to clear both.

stories.title: Go Container — Container Runtime from Scratch
stories.situation: I built Go Container, a container runtime in Go, to deeply understand what Docker and similar tools actually do at the Linux kernel level.
stories.task: This meant implementing process isolation from first principles — no containerisation libraries, just the Linux kernel primitives.
stories.action: I used Linux namespaces to give each container its own process space, filesystem, and hostname. I used cgroups to cap process counts and automatically clean up after container exit. I set up chroot and mounted pseudo-filesystems to create complete filesystem separation from the host. Each layer was tested independently before integration.
stories.result: A functional container runtime that can isolate processes, enforce resource limits, and maintain filesystem separation from the host. Building it from scratch gave me a depth of understanding of Linux internals and containerisation that reading Docker documentation never would have.

question: How do you stay up-to-date with the latest technological advancements?

stories.title: Open Source as Active Learning
stories.situation: Reading about new technologies is passive. Contributing to production open source systems is active — you have to understand the code deeply enough to change it correctly.
stories.task: I use open source contributions as my primary method of staying current with how production systems evolve.
stories.action: I actively contribute to facebook/react, apache/kafka, pgjdbc, and kubernetes-client/java. When React 19 shipped reconciler changes, I had to read the source to understand them — not just a blog post. When I merged a PR into pgjdbc, I had to understand PostgreSQL's connection protocol at a byte level.
stories.result: I have PRs merged into systems used by developers at JPMorgan, LinkedIn, and Meta. I learn technologies at the level of production maintainers, not tutorial consumers. That gap matters in interviews and on the job.

stories.title: Building from Scratch to Learn Internals
stories.situation: When I wanted to understand databases and containers deeply, I didn't read documentation — I built NanoSQL and Go Container from scratch.
stories.task: You can't stay current by just reading about technology. You have to build with it.
