stories.action: For NanoSQL I implemented B-tree indexing, persistent storage, and custom memory management in C — because understanding SQLite's internals makes me a better database user. For Go Container I implemented Linux namespaces and cgroups directly — because understanding what Docker does at the kernel level makes me a better infrastructure engineer.
stories.result: I now use PostgreSQL, Docker, and Kafka with a depth of understanding that comes from having built equivalent systems. That foundation makes it much faster to learn the next tool in any of those domains.

stories.title: Following Real Codebase Evolution
stories.situation: Most 'stay current' advice involves reading newsletters and watching conference talks. My approach is to follow the actual codebases of major projects through their commit history and RFCs.
stories.task: I want to understand why technology evolves, not just what changed.
stories.action: I followed the React 19 RFC process from proposal to implementation, read the Kafka KIP process for consumer group changes, and subscribe to PostgreSQL mailing list summaries. When I see a change, I read the PR that implemented it — not just the release notes.
stories.result: This gives me about 6-12 months of lead time on where the ecosystem is moving, because RFCs and open issues tell you what's coming before it ships. In my React 19 open source work, I found the StrictMode regression because I was following the reconciler changes closely enough to notice the behaviour had changed.

question: Give an example of a time you had to debug a challenging technical issue.

stories.title: Kafka Consumer Latency at Ericsson
stories.situation: Two weeks before launch at Ericsson, load testing showed p99 latency at 380ms against our 200ms SLA — and the cause wasn't obvious.
stories.task: I needed to find and fix the bottleneck without making random changes that might introduce new issues.
stories.action: I methodically isolated variables. First I ruled out network — inter-broker latency was fine. Then I checked producer throughput — normal. Then I looked at consumer group rebalancing frequency in the broker logs and found it was happening every 15-30 seconds under load. I traced it to our session timeout being too short for the processing time of high-volume batches, triggering false-positive timeouts and rebalances. I switched to sticky partition assignment and tuned the heartbeat interval.
stories.result: Latency dropped to under 180ms. Systematic isolation — ruling out one layer at a time — found the root cause in a day. The lesson: in distributed systems debugging, eliminate entire layers before going deep on any one layer.

stories.title: React 19 StrictMode Regression
stories.situation: While working on my open source React contribution, I noticed that certain component effects were firing three times in StrictMode in React 19, where React 18 fired them twice — a behaviour change not documented in the migration guide.
stories.task: I needed to determine if this was intentional, a regression, or expected new behaviour — with enough evidence to open a credible issue.
stories.action: I git-bisected the React 19 commits to find the exact commit that changed the behaviour. I wrote a minimal reproduction case — 20 lines of code that demonstrated the issue without any other dependencies. I then traced the relevant reconciler code path in the source to understand the mechanism. I opened a detailed issue with the bisection commit, the reproduction case, and the code path.
stories.result: Maintainers confirmed it was an unintentional regression. My PR targeting the fix is active. Rigorous debugging — bisection, minimal reproduction, source tracing — is what separates a credible open source issue from noise.

stories.title: MQTT Intermittent Message Loss at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, after deploying the IoT pipeline, we started seeing intermittent message loss — maybe 1-2% of sensor readings were missing in PostgreSQL, but we couldn't reproduce it reliably.
stories.task: Intermittent bugs are the hardest kind — the failure doesn't happen when you're watching.
stories.action: I added structured logging at every pipeline stage — MQTT ingestion, transformation, and PostgreSQL insert — with message IDs I could trace end-to-end. I ran the pipeline under sustained load in staging for 24 hours and collected the logs. I found that message loss always correlated with PostgreSQL connection pool exhaustion under burst load — the connection pool was too small for our peak volume.
stories.result: I increased the connection pool size and added a queue buffer in front of the PostgreSQL writer. Message loss dropped to zero in subsequent tests. The lesson: intermittent bugs require observation infrastructure first — you can't debug what you can't see.

question: Why are you interested in working at [company name]?

stories.title: For a Systems / Infrastructure Company
stories.situation: I've spent the last two years building systems at the infrastructure level — Kafka microservices at Ericsson, IoT pipelines at Noon, a container runtime and database engine in personal projects.
stories.task: I'm looking for a company where that depth of systems thinking is valued and where the engineering problems are genuinely hard.
stories.action: Your team's work on [specific system/product] is exactly the kind of problem I want to work on. The [specific technical challenge they solve] requires the kind of distributed systems thinking I've been developing — and I want to do it at a scale where the engineering decisions have real consequences.
stories.result: I'm bringing production experience with Kafka, Kubernetes, and event-driven systems, open source contributions to production-grade codebases, and the habit of going to first principles. I want to grow at a company where that approach is matched by the people around me.

stories.title: For a Product / Consumer Company
stories.situation: At Ericsson I built a React dashboard that reduced a 30-engineer team's debugging time from 60 to 10 minutes. At Noon I built a notification system delivering updates in under 10ms. In both cases the most satisfying part was seeing engineers and users actually change their behaviour because of what I built.
stories.task: I want to work on products that people use and care about — where the feedback loop between engineering decisions and real user outcomes is short and clear.
stories.action: Your product reaches [X users / market segment] and the engineering challenge of making it fast, reliable, and scalable at that level is exactly what I want to work on. I've followed [specific product decision or engineering blog post] and it shows the kind of engineering rigour I want to be part of.
stories.result: I want to build things that matter at scale. Based on what I've read about your engineering culture and the problems you're solving, I think this is where I can do that.

stories.title: For a Healthcare / Mission-Driven Company
stories.situation: I built MedStroke AI and piloted it at a Louisiana hospital. Watching it reduce time-to-treatment by 35% — and knowing that matters for patient outcomes — was a completely different feeling than shipping a dashboard metric.
stories.task: I want to work on technology where the stakes are real and the mission is something I'd work on even if it were harder than the alternatives.
stories.action: Your work on [specific healthcare/mission product] is solving a problem that matters in the same way. The technical challenges — reliability, data sensitivity, regulatory considerations — are harder than most software problems, and I think that's what makes them worth working on.
stories.result: I have experience building software that clinicians and real users depend on, and I want to keep doing work where the engineering quality directly affects outcomes for real people.

question: Assume you are given a task to design a system. How would you do it? How would you resolve ambiguity?

stories.title: Ericsson Event-Driven System Design
stories.situation: At Ericsson I was asked to design a system to aggregate 10K+ pricing records from multiple upstream sources into RESTful endpoints — with minimal context on volume, latency requirements, or upstream formats.
stories.task: Before writing a line of code I needed to resolve the ambiguity in requirements and produce a design I could defend.
stories.action: I started with clarifying questions: What's the acceptable latency for a price update to appear downstream? What happens if an upstream source goes down? How stale can data be? From those answers I understood we needed near-real-time (not eventual consistency) and fault tolerance. I then chose event streaming over REST polling because of the latency and decoupling requirements, drew a data flow diagram, identified the failure modes, and presented it for review before building.
stories.result: The design was approved and the system shipped — aggregating 10K+ records into RESTful endpoints with real-time access to previously disconnected historical data. Resolving ambiguity means asking the right questions, not making assumptions.

stories.title: MedStroke System Architecture
stories.situation: When I started MedStroke, the only clear requirement was 'a stroke diagnosis tool for hospitals.' Everything else — data model, user roles, real-time vs batch, ML integration — was undefined.
stories.task: I needed to turn a vague goal into a concrete system design.
stories.action: I did clinical context research first — how do stroke diagnoses actually happen? That told me real-time monitoring was essential, roles (neurologist vs nurse) determined data access, and the ML output needed to be explainable, not just accurate. I drew the component diagram, identified the highest-risk integration points (ML model → clinical display), and validated the design with the hospital contact before building.
stories.result: The design held through the full development cycle with only minor scope adjustments. Clinical context research was the most important step — the system needed to fit a workflow that already existed, not invent one.

stories.title: NanoSQL Database Design
stories.situation: Building NanoSQL — a SQLite clone in C — required designing a full database engine from a blank page: storage format, indexing strategy, query execution, and memory management.
stories.task: I had to make foundational design decisions that would determine everything downstream.
stories.action: I read the SQLite design document and the original B-tree paper before writing any code. I started with the storage layer — page format and persistence — because every other component depended on it. I designed each layer's interface before implementing it, so I could test components independently. I wrote the B-tree specification in pseudocode first, which revealed three edge cases I hadn't thought through.
stories.result: The system delivered B-tree indexing, persistent storage, and SQL operations. Writing the specification before the implementation found more bugs than testing did. For any complex system: design the interfaces first, then implement, then test.

question: Have you ever been in a situation where another team and yours were creating a similar product? What happened?

stories.title: Duplicate PR in Open Source
stories.situation: While working on a fix for a pgjdbc issue, I discovered that another contributor had opened a PR solving the same problem two days before me — with a slightly different approach.
stories.task: I needed to decide whether to close my PR, continue it, or find a path that incorporated the best of both.
stories.action: I read their implementation carefully, identified one case my approach handled better (connection timeout edge case), commented on their PR pointing it out constructively, and offered to either contribute that fix to their PR or merge our approaches. I wasn't attached to my PR getting the credit.
stories.result: The maintainer suggested I add my edge case handling to their PR, which I did. It merged with both our contributions credited. Open source collaboration works best when you're optimising for the best outcome, not whose name is on the commit.

stories.title: Parallel Pipeline Work at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, I discovered midway through my project that another team had been building a separate data ingestion pipeline with partial overlap in functionality with mine.
stories.task: We were duplicating infrastructure effort and potentially creating competing systems that would both need maintenance.
stories.action: I flagged the overlap to my manager and requested a sync with the other team. We mapped out what each pipeline did and found the overlap was in the ingestion layer — both were consuming from similar upstream sources. We agreed to standardise on shared Kafka topics with different consumer groups rather than maintaining two separate ingestion paths.
stories.result: The teams consolidated the ingestion infrastructure, reducing maintenance burden for both. The key was raising the overlap early — a month later it would have been much harder to change. When you see duplication of effort, say something immediately.

question: What is the biggest technical challenge you have worked on?

stories.title: Kafka at 400K Events/Day with Sub-200ms SLA
stories.situation: At Ericsson, the hardest technical challenge was building a Kafka microservice that processed 400K+ daily events across 6 topics with a hard sub-200ms latency requirement — in a production Kubernetes environment I was new to.
stories.task: The challenge wasn't just throughput — it was throughput plus latency plus fault tolerance plus observability, all at once.
stories.action: I front-loaded the riskiest technical work: consumer group configuration and latency profiling. I used sticky partition assignment to prevent unnecessary rebalancing, tuned heartbeat intervals and session timeouts under simulated load, and built load tests that ran at 2x expected volume. I found and fixed the rebalancing bug two weeks before launch.
stories.result: Sub-200ms latency from day one. The challenge taught me that distributed systems performance requires understanding the failure modes first — then designing around them — not optimising after the fact.

stories.title: React 19 Reconciler — Open Source Contribution
stories.situation: Finding and contributing a fix for a React 19 StrictMode regression in the reconciler is the most technically challenging open source work I've done.
stories.task: The React reconciler is among the most complex JavaScript ever written — concurrent rendering, work-in-progress fibers, priority scheduling — and I had to understand it well enough to propose a correct fix.
stories.action: I git-bisected 200+ commits to find the regression, read the fiber reconciler source to understand the StrictMode double-invoke mechanism, wrote a minimal 20-line reproduction case, and proposed a fix with a regression test. I documented every step in the PR description so maintainers could validate my reasoning.
stories.result: Maintainers confirmed the regression and the PR is active. This was the biggest technical challenge not because of the code volume but because of the precision required — a wrong fix in the React reconciler affects every React application in production.

stories.title: Building a Container Runtime from Linux Primitives
stories.situation: Go Container was my most challenging personal project — implementing container isolation from first principles using Linux kernel primitives with no containerisation libraries.
stories.task: I needed to implement the actual isolation mechanisms: process namespace separation, filesystem isolation, and resource limits — without Docker or any abstraction layer.
stories.action: I used Linux clone() with CLONE_NEWPID, CLONE_NEWNS, CLONE_NEWUTS flags for namespace isolation, cgroups v2 for resource controls, and chroot with pseudo-filesystem mounting for filesystem isolation. I read kernel documentation — not Go documentation — to understand the underlying mechanisms. I tested each isolation layer independently.
stories.result: A working container runtime that can isolate processes and enforce resource limits. The challenge was that kernel documentation assumes knowledge of C systems programming, not Go. Building at that level of abstraction means reading primary sources and being comfortable with things that are not in tutorials.

question: Why do you want to change your current company?

stories.title: After Internship / Apprenticeship
stories.situation: My Ericsson apprenticeship was a genuinely great experience — I shipped a Kafka microservice processing 400K+ daily events, built observability tooling that helped 30 engineers, and learned distributed systems in a real production environment.
stories.task: I'm looking for a full-time role that builds on that foundation with new scale, new problem domains, and engineers I can learn from.
stories.action: I'm specifically looking for a role where I can go deeper on [distributed systems / ML / product engineering], work with a team that has strong engineering culture, and take on more ownership than an apprenticeship allows.
stories.result: What I built at Ericsson taught me what I'm capable of. I want to find out what's next — and I believe this role is the right environment to do that.

stories.title: Seeking Greater Scope and Impact
stories.situation: Both at Ericsson and Noon I shipped systems with real impact — 400K daily events, 40% downtime reduction, 35% faster stroke treatment at a hospital. Each time, the most motivating moment was seeing real users change their behaviour because of what I built.
stories.task: I want a role where the scope of impact is larger and the engineering challenges are harder.
stories.action: I've been deliberate about what I build outside of work — open source contributions to facebook/react and apache/kafka, systems projects in Go and C, a healthcare AI tool piloted at a real hospital. All of that points toward wanting to work on systems that matter at scale.
stories.result: I'm not leaving for a small reason. I'm moving toward a problem I'm genuinely excited about, at a company whose engineering culture matches how I want to work.

stories.title: Student Seeking First Full-Time Role
stories.situation: I'm graduating from Grambling State in May 2028 with a 3.93 GPA, two production internships, open source contributions, and projects ranging from a container runtime to a healthcare AI system.
stories.task: I'm looking for a full-time role that's genuinely challenging — not a role where I'll be comfortable within six months.
stories.action: I've been intentional about the kind of engineer I'm building toward — systems-level depth, production-scale experience, and open source contribution. I've sought out hard problems rather than comfortable ones. This role is aligned with that trajectory.
stories.result: I want to join a team where I'll be learning from the people around me for years, not months. Based on what I know about your team, that's what I'd get here.

question: Tell me a time when you had a different opinion than the rest of the team. How did you handle it?

stories.title: Event-Driven vs Traditional at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, when I proposed using Apache Kafka for the pricing system, the initial team preference was a simpler REST polling approach — Kafka felt unnecessarily complex for the team at the time.
stories.task: I believed strongly that at 400K events/day with sub-200ms latency requirements, REST polling would not meet our SLA and would create load on upstream services. I needed to make that case without bulldozing team consensus.
stories.action: I built a small benchmark rather than arguing in the abstract. I simulated both approaches under load and showed the latency numbers. I acknowledged the operational complexity Kafka would add and offered to own that complexity personally.
stories.result: The team agreed to Kafka after seeing the data. The system hit sub-200ms latency from launch. Having a different opinion from the team is fine — what matters is bringing evidence, not just conviction.

stories.title: Random Forest vs Neural Network for MedStroke
stories.situation: On MedStroke, my teammate preferred a neural network for the stroke risk model. I advocated for Random Forest, believing interpretability was essential in a clinical context.
stories.task: This was a genuine disagreement and both approaches had real technical merit — it wasn't obvious who was right.
stories.action: I framed the decision around the clinical user. 'A neurologist needs to be able to challenge the model's reasoning. How do we explain a neural network's prediction to a doctor in 30 seconds?' Random Forest's feature importance scores gave us that. I put the use case first, not my preference.
stories.result: We chose Random Forest. The doctors engaged with the confidence scores and feature outputs constructively. My different opinion was accepted because it was grounded in user need, not technical preference.

stories.title: Multiple Partitions vs Single Partition at Ericsson
stories.situation: When designing the Kafka consumer topology at Ericsson, the team's initial instinct was single partitions for simplicity — easier to reason about, simpler to operate.
stories.task: I was the only one arguing for multiple partitions to support parallelism, and I was the most junior person in the discussion.
stories.action: I ran the throughput math and showed it to the team without editorialising. 'At 400K events/day, single partition consumers will have X minutes of lag at peak. Here's the calculation.' The data spoke rather than me asserting I was right.
stories.result: We moved to multiple partitions. The system scaled cleanly. Being junior doesn't mean your opinion doesn't count — but it means you need to back it with data twice as thoroughly.

question: Tell me about a time when you were faced with a problem that had a number of possible solutions. What was the problem and how did you determine the course of action?

stories.title: Ericsson Latency Problem — Multiple Possible Fixes
stories.situation: When Ericsson load tests showed 380ms latency against our 200ms SLA, there were at least four plausible causes: network overhead, producer batch size, consumer processing time, and consumer group rebalancing.
stories.task: I needed to identify the right fix without trying all four simultaneously — which would make it impossible to know what worked.
stories.action: I isolated one variable at a time. I measured network latency first — negligible. Producer batch size — already optimal. Consumer processing time — within bounds. Consumer group rebalancing frequency — abnormally high under load. I'd found the root cause methodically. I fixed the partition assignor configuration and session timeout.
stories.result: Latency dropped to under 180ms. The systematic isolation approach found the fix in one day. When there are multiple possible solutions, the right process is elimination — not trying everything and seeing what sticks.

stories.title: MedStroke ML Model Selection
stories.situation: For MedStroke AI's stroke risk prediction, I evaluated five possible approaches: logistic regression, Random Forest, gradient boosting, SVM, and a small neural network.
stories.task: With our dataset size, timeline, and clinical interpretability requirement, I needed a principled way to choose.
stories.action: I scored each option against three criteria: interpretability for doctors, accuracy given our dataset size, and implementation time within our timeline. I ran baseline experiments on each. Gradient boosting edged Random Forest on accuracy but was harder to explain. Logistic regression was too simple. Random Forest hit the right tradeoff across all three criteria.
stories.result: Random Forest shipped. The decision framework — explicit criteria weighted by use case constraints — gave me a defensible choice rather than a preference. I now use this approach for any decision with multiple viable options.

stories.title: IoT Pipeline Architecture at Noon
stories.situation: When designing the Noon IoT pipeline, I had three viable options: a batch ETL approach, a REST polling approach, or MQTT with Kafka Connect.
stories.task: Each had real tradeoffs and the right choice depended on latency requirements and device count — both of which were partially unclear at the time.
stories.action: I wrote a one-page decision doc mapping each option against the requirements I was confident in: device count (large), network reliability (variable), PostgreSQL as the sink (fixed). MQTT with Kafka Connect handled variable network conditions best and scaled to device count without polling overhead. I presented the doc and got quick sign-off.
stories.result: The MQTT/Kafka Connect pipeline shipped and reduced downtime detection by 40%. Writing the decision doc forced me to make the requirements explicit — which clarified the decision faster than any technical debate would have.

question: Describe a time when you needed to motivate a group of individuals or encourage collaboration during a particular project.

stories.title: NSBE Chapter Revitalisation
stories.situation: When I became NSBE Assistant VP, member engagement was low — people showed up to one event and didn't come back. The chapter had a reputation for being only loosely technical.
stories.task: I needed to change the culture and give people a reason to show up consistently.
stories.action: I surveyed members on what they actually needed — most said interview prep and peer community. I restructured our event calendar around those two things. I created a mentorship pairing system between senior students and freshmen. I celebrated every placement and internship offer publicly in the chapter — making progress visible motivated the people who hadn't got there yet.
stories.result: Membership grew to 300+ active members. Retention improved significantly. The key insight was that motivation follows relevance — people engage consistently when the community is directly solving their actual problems.

stories.title: MedStroke Team Through ML Struggles
stories.situation: Midway through MedStroke development, our ML model accuracy was stuck at 35% and the team's energy was flagging. The hospital pilot deadline was fixed.
stories.task: I needed to keep the team motivated and focused during a period where progress felt invisible.
stories.action: I shifted how I measured progress — instead of accuracy as the only metric, I highlighted every other thing going well: the backend was solid, the UI was clean, the NIHSS scoring was clinically accurate. I broke the ML problem into smaller experiments, each with its own result to report at standup. Progress became visible again even when the headline number wasn't moving.
stories.result: The team kept moving and we shipped. The hospital pilot reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. When motivation dips in technical projects, the fix is usually making progress visible — not giving speeches.

stories.title: Cross-Functional Collaboration at Ericsson
stories.situation: The Ericsson React dashboard required input from engineers across the team — each had strong opinions about what data mattered and limited time to engage.
stories.task: I needed to get useful input from busy engineers without making it feel like another meeting.
stories.action: I sent a one-page async survey with three specific questions — 'What's the first metric you check during an incident?' — and followed up individually with the two engineers whose answers were most detailed. I incorporated their input visibly and told them I had, which motivated continued engagement.
stories.result: The dashboard was shaped by real user needs rather than my assumptions. Engineers adopted it immediately because it showed what they actually cared about. Getting collaboration from busy people means making it easy and showing that their input mattered.

question: What do you do to enhance your technical knowledge apart from your project work?

stories.title: Open Source Contributions
stories.situation: I contribute to facebook/react, apache/kafka, pgjdbc, and kubernetes-client/java — production systems used by millions of developers and companies.
stories.task: Contributing to these codebases requires understanding them at a depth that tutorials and documentation don't provide.
stories.action: I find real bugs, study the source code to understand the root cause, write reproduction cases, propose fixes with tests, and engage with maintainer feedback. I've had PRs merged into pgjdbc and kubernetes-client/java, and have an active PR against the React 19 reconciler targeting a confirmed StrictMode regression.
stories.result: I now understand how PostgreSQL manages connections at the protocol level, how Kafka consumers manage offsets under the hood, and how React's reconciler handles concurrent rendering. That depth of understanding compounds — every production system I use, I understand better than its documentation.

stories.title: Building Systems from First Principles
stories.situation: Beyond tutorials and courses, I build systems from scratch specifically to understand what higher-level tools abstract away.
stories.task: This is the most direct path to deep technical knowledge — you can't fake understanding when you have to implement it.
stories.action: I built NanoSQL — a SQLite clone in C with B-tree indexing and persistent storage — to understand how databases actually work. I built Go Container — a container runtime using Linux namespaces and cgroups — to understand what Docker actually does. Both projects required reading primary sources: database papers, Linux kernel documentation, language specifications.
stories.result: I use PostgreSQL, Docker, and Kafka with a depth of understanding that makes me faster to debug, better at system design, and more valuable in code reviews. First-principles projects are the fastest path to real expertise.

stories.title: Following Primary Sources in Real Codebases
stories.situation: Most engineers follow newsletters and blog posts to stay current. I follow the actual codebases, RFCs, and issue trackers of major projects.
stories.task: Blog posts tell you what changed. Source code and RFCs tell you why — which is what matters for engineering decisions.
stories.action: I followed the React 19 RFC process from proposal to reconciler implementation. I read Kafka KIPs for consumer group protocol changes. I subscribe to PostgreSQL commit digests. When something changes, I read the PR that introduced it — not just the release notes.
stories.result: This gives me 6-12 months of lead time on where major tools are moving, because I'm reading the discussions while they're still open. It also means when I contribute open source work, I understand the codebase well enough to write fixes that match the maintainers' intent.

question: How do you prioritize your workload? What do you do when your work feels like it's just too much to get done?

stories.title: Three Concurrent Ericsson Deliverables
stories.situation: At Ericsson I had three concurrent deliverables: the Kafka microservice, the event-driven pricing system in F#, and the React dashboard — all with overlapping timelines.
stories.task: I had to sequence them without any of the three slipping.
stories.action: I identified the external dependencies for each: the Kafka microservice had an upstream team dependency that made it the critical path. I prioritised it first. The F# system had a fixed integration test date. I time-boxed its daily work to protect the Kafka development. The dashboard had no external dependencies — I worked on it in smaller blocks. I communicated my prioritisation to my manager explicitly so there were no surprises.
stories.result: All three shipped. Sub-200ms latency on the microservice, real-time pricing data access on the F# system, and 60→10 minute debugging improvement on the dashboard. Prioritisation is a communication act as much as a planning act — your manager needs to know what you're deprioritising and why.

stories.title: When Work Feels Overwhelming
stories.situation: During the MedStroke hospital pilot sprint, I hit a point where the ML model, the API, the frontend, and the clinical documentation all felt urgent simultaneously and I couldn't see a path through.
stories.task: Overwhelm is a signal that I've lost clarity on what's actually critical — not that there's too much work.
stories.action: I stopped and listed every task in one place. Then I applied one question: 'What breaks the hospital pilot if it's not done?' That list was four items. Everything else got explicitly deferred for the sprint. I told the team what I was deferring and why, so no one was surprised.
stories.result: The pilot launched on time. The deferred items were delivered in the following sprint with no negative consequence. Overwhelm comes from trying to hold everything in your head simultaneously — the fix is externalising the list and then being ruthless about what's truly on the critical path.

stories.title: School + NSBE + Applications
stories.situation: Balancing 18 credit hours, NSBE leadership responsibilities, and a competitive internship application cycle simultaneously felt genuinely unsustainable at one point.
stories.task: I needed a system that would let me make progress on all three without burning out or dropping any of them.
stories.action: I gave each priority a fixed weekly time budget rather than letting them compete in real time. NSBE got Sundays and Tuesday evenings — capped. Coursework got morning blocks — non-negotiable. Applications were batched to Saturday mornings. When something tried to expand outside its budget, I pushed back rather than absorbing it. I also identified which NSBE tasks could be delegated to other officers.
stories.result: 3.93 GPA. NSBE grew to 300+ members. Ericsson offer. Fixed budgets and delegation are the answer to unsustainable workloads — not working more hours.

question: What's the number one accomplishment you're most proud of?

stories.title: Ericsson Kafka Pipeline — 400K Events
stories.situation: Building the Kafka-streaming microservice at Ericsson that processes 400K+ daily events across 6 topics with sub-200ms latency is the technical accomplishment I'm most proud of.
stories.task: It replaced manual SQL workflows, required solving real distributed systems problems under production SLAs, and was fully shipped within an internship window.
stories.action: I designed the consumer group topology, tuned partition assignment and heartbeat intervals under load, built load tests at 2x production volume, found and fixed a rebalancing bug two weeks before launch, and built a React dashboard to make the system observable.
stories.result: 400K+ daily events at sub-200ms latency, replacing an entire class of manual work for the team. It's the accomplishment I'm most proud of technically because it required understanding distributed systems at a depth that courses don't teach — only production experience does.

stories.title: MedStroke — 35% Faster Stroke Treatment
stories.situation: Piloting MedStroke AI at a Louisiana hospital and watching it reduce time-to-treatment by 35% is the accomplishment I'm most proud of from an impact perspective.
stories.task: Time-to-treatment in stroke care directly affects patient outcomes — faster treatment means less brain damage. Building something that moved that number is a different kind of impact than shipping a feature.
stories.action: I built the full system — ML pipeline, FastAPI backend, React frontend, NIHSS scoring, tPA eligibility automation — and managed the hospital stakeholder relationship through a genuinely skeptical clinical team.
stories.result: 35% faster time-to-treatment. The accomplishment I'm most proud of isn't the technical build — it's that doctors who were skeptical of AI tools actually adopted it and changed their workflow. That's harder to achieve than any engineering challenge.

stories.title: NSBE — 300+ Member Community
stories.situation: Growing NSBE at Grambling from low attendance to 300+ active members and $3K in corporate partnerships is the accomplishment I'm most proud of in terms of community impact.
stories.task: Engineering communities don't grow automatically — someone has to do the unglamorous work of outreach, event planning, and relationship building.
stories.action: I restructured the event calendar around real member needs, built a mentorship system, personally cold-contacted 12 companies for sponsorships, and made every member placement and offer visible and celebrated within the chapter.
stories.result: 300+ active members. $3K in partnerships. Students getting access to opportunities they wouldn't have found otherwise. I'm proud of it because it required skills I had to develop — public speaking, sales, community organising — and the impact is compounding. Those students will pay it forward.

question: Tell me about a time when you had an excessive amount of work and you knew you could not meet the deadline. How did you manage?

stories.title: MedStroke Beta Testing Feedback Flood
stories.situation: One week before the hospital pilot, we received extensive feedback from the beta clinical testers — the UI needed significant changes and two backend flows had edge cases that failed in real clinical scenarios.
stories.task: There was no way to address all of it in one week at the quality bar I'd set. I had to decide what to do.
stories.action: I triaged the feedback into three buckets: blocks the pilot (must fix), degrades the pilot (should fix), and nice to have (defer). I communicated the triage to the team immediately, set realistic expectations with the hospital contact, and focused every hour on the blockers. I was honest that two 'should fix' items would be in the first post-pilot update, not the launch.
stories.result: The pilot launched with all blockers resolved. The hospital contact appreciated the transparency — they preferred an honest conversation to a surprise. The two deferred items shipped in the following week. Managing the impossible deadline means triaging transparently, not trying to do everything badly.

stories.title: Ericsson Sprint Overcommitment
stories.situation: At Ericsson, I accepted more scope in a sprint than I could realistically deliver — I underestimated the F# learning curve and the Kafka tuning work simultaneously.
stories.task: I realised three days in that I was going to miss commitments. The question was how to handle it.
stories.action: I told my manager immediately — not at the end of the sprint. I explained what I'd underestimated and offered two options: drop the F# work to this sprint and carry the Kafka tuning, or reverse. My manager chose — the decision was theirs to make with full information. I didn't pretend I could somehow do both.
stories.result: We re-scoped cleanly. Both pieces shipped in the right sprint. Telling your manager early about a slip costs you some discomfort. Telling them late costs the whole team time. Early transparency is always the right call.

stories.title: NSBE Semester Overcommitment
stories.situation: In one semester I'd committed to organising three large NSBE events, carrying 18 credit hours, and completing a personal project with a self-imposed deadline. Two weeks in I knew it was too much.
stories.task: Something had to give — I needed to decide what and communicate it.
stories.action: I identified which NSBE events had external dependencies that made them non-negotiable (the career fair already had companies registered) and which I could scale back or delegate. I delegated two tasks to other officers with clear ownership. I dropped the personal project timeline rather than drop my coursework or the external-facing NSBE commitments.
stories.result: The career fair ran successfully, my GPA held, and the personal project shipped a month later. Knowing when to say 'this timeline isn't real' and adjusting early is a skill — not a failure.

question: What will be your course of action if you are assigned a task you don't know at all?

stories.title: Learning F# at Ericsson from Zero
stories.situation: At Ericsson I was assigned to build an event-driven system in F# — a functional language I had never written a line of.
stories.task: I had to get productive in an unfamiliar language quickly while delivering a production system on a real timeline.
stories.action: First, I was upfront with my manager about the learning curve and gave a realistic revised estimate. Then I spent the first five days fully on learning — F# documentation, async/await patterns, functional data transformation. I built a small throwaway prototype to validate my understanding before touching production code. When I got stuck, I asked specific, targeted questions rather than broad ones.
stories.result: I delivered the F# system on time — aggregating 10K+ pricing records into RESTful endpoints. The key was being honest about the ramp, investing in learning before building, and asking precise questions. Pretending to know something you don't is the most expensive mistake in engineering.

stories.title: Learning MQTT Protocol at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon I was assigned to build an IoT pipeline using MQTT — a protocol I'd never worked with.
stories.task: I needed to design a reliable production system around a technology I was starting from zero on.
stories.action: I read the MQTT specification before writing any code — specifically the QoS levels, message ordering guarantees, and reconnection protocol. I built a small local test harness with a real MQTT broker to validate my understanding of edge cases before integrating it into the pipeline. I also looked at the Kafka Connect MQTT connector documentation and source.
stories.result: The pipeline shipped with correct MQTT QoS configuration and reconnection handling. The load reduced downtime detection by 40%. Learning a new protocol correctly from the spec is faster than learning it incorrectly from examples — you avoid a class of bugs that are nearly impossible to debug after the fact.

stories.title: React 19 Reconciler — Open Source
stories.situation: When I found the React 19 StrictMode regression, investigating it required understanding the React reconciler — code I'd never read before and that has no beginner-friendly documentation.
stories.task: I had to understand it well enough to identify a root cause and propose a fix.
stories.action: I started by reading the most recent reconciler RFCs to understand the design intent. Then I used git-bisection to narrow the problem to a specific commit rather than reading all the code. Once I had the specific commit, I read only the changed lines and the surrounding context. I wrote a minimal reproduction case to validate my understanding before proposing any fix.
stories.result: I identified the root cause and opened a PR with a regression test. The maintainers confirmed the issue. Approaching complex unknown code: bisect to narrow, read the minimum necessary, validate with a reproduction case before proposing a solution.

question: Give an example of when you took a huge risk and failed.

stories.title: Over-Ambitious ML Target for MedStroke
stories.situation: Before properly assessing our dataset quality and size, I publicly committed to 80% accuracy for MedStroke's stroke risk model — to the team and to the hospital contact.
stories.task: The commitment was made with confidence but without sufficient validation of whether the dataset could support that target.
stories.action: I worked intensively on the model — SMOTE balancing, feature engineering, hyperparameter tuning, cross-validation. Every technique I tried improved the model, but the ceiling was 45-48% given our dataset size. At that point I was out of time and options within the project constraints.
stories.result: I missed the target I'd committed to — publicly and by a significant margin. I was transparent immediately, reframed the tool appropriately, and the hospital pilot still ran successfully. The lesson I carry from this: never commit to an accuracy target before validating your dataset size and quality. Confidence should follow evidence, not precede it.

stories.title: NanoSQL Custom Page Allocator
stories.situation: In NanoSQL I decided to build a custom page allocator instead of using standard malloc — I was convinced I could reduce fragmentation and improve B-tree performance.
stories.task: This was ambitious for a solo project with a self-imposed deadline, and the allocator was not on the critical path for the project's core goals.
stories.action: I invested a full week building the allocator. It worked for simple sequential operations but introduced non-deterministic corruption bugs under mixed read/write workloads — the kind that manifested hours into a test run, not immediately.
stories.result: I scrapped the allocator entirely and used standard C memory management. The B-tree became stable and shipped. The risk failed because I added complexity that wasn't needed for the goal. The lesson: in systems programming, custom memory management is almost never worth the risk unless you have a profiler showing it's the bottleneck.

stories.title: React Dashboard Prediction Feature
stories.situation: When building the Ericsson React dashboard, I decided to add a predictive analytics component that would forecast consumer lag before it became critical.
stories.task: I allocated three days to this feature, believing I could implement a simple time-series model against the pipeline metrics.
stories.action: The prediction required historical baseline data we hadn't been collecting — without it, any prediction was noise. I spent three days before accepting the feature wasn't going to be credible without the historical data foundation.
stories.result: I cut the feature entirely. The core dashboard shipped on time and cut debugging time from 60 to 10 minutes without it. The risk failed because I didn't validate the data dependency before starting the build. Now I check data availability before scoping any analytics feature.

question: Describe a time when you had to work simultaneously on both high-priority urgent projects as well as long-term projects. How did you handle both?

stories.title: Production Bug vs Kafka Feature at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, midway through building the Kafka microservice (long-term), a production pricing data issue was reported in an earlier component — event ordering was broken and affecting live reporting.
stories.task: The production issue was urgent and high-severity. The Kafka microservice had a hard launch deadline. Both needed my attention.
stories.action: I triaged the production issue first to confirm severity — it was affecting live business reporting, which made it the immediate priority. I communicated the context switch to my manager explicitly. I fixed the ordering issue, validated in staging, deployed, then wrote up a postmortem before returning to the microservice. I adjusted my microservice timeline by one day and communicated that proactively.
stories.result: Production restored within the day. Microservice launched on schedule with the one-day adjustment. The key was explicit communication at every switch — no one was surprised by where my time was going.

stories.title: Hospital Pilot Prep + New Feature Build
stories.situation: During MedStroke development, I was simultaneously preparing the system for the hospital pilot (urgent — fixed date) and building new NIHSS scoring features for the full product roadmap.
stories.task: I couldn't let pilot prep slip, but stopping all feature work for two weeks would set the roadmap back significantly.
stories.action: I allocated mornings to the pilot-critical work — testing, clinical validation, documentation — and afternoons to roadmap features. I set a rule: any bug discovered in pilot prep immediately preempted afternoon feature work. I was transparent with the team about this prioritisation so everyone understood why some features were moving slowly.
stories.result: The pilot launched fully prepared. Two roadmap features shipped during that window at a slower pace. The time-box approach — mornings for urgent, afternoons for long-term — kept both moving without either consuming the other.

stories.title: NSBE Career Fair + Semester Research Project
stories.situation: As NSBE Assistant VP, I was running our biggest career fair (urgent, external companies registered, fixed date) while simultaneously completing a semester-long systems research project with a final submission date.
stories.task: Both had hard deadlines within the same two-week window.
stories.action: I mapped the dependencies. Career fair logistics were front-loaded — confirmations, room setup, communication — and mostly done by day 5. Research project writing was back-loaded. I scheduled the career fair work for the first week, the research project for the second, and gave myself one overlap day as buffer. I delegated two career fair tasks to other officers.
stories.result: Career fair ran successfully with 300+ member attendance. Research project submitted on time. Sequential rather than parallel execution is often faster when both tasks require deep focus — the context switching cost is real.

question: Tell me about a time when you had a hard time working with someone in your team. How did you handle it?

stories.title: Async-Only Teammate on MedStroke
stories.situation: On MedStroke, one teammate did excellent work but communicated only asynchronously — no standups, no check-ins — and their component was on the critical path to the hospital pilot.
stories.task: I needed to understand their progress without micromanaging or damaging a working relationship with someone who was technically strong.
stories.action: I had a direct one-on-one — not a group conversation — and said clearly: 'I'm not getting enough signal on where the backend integration stands, and I'm worried about the pilot timeline. Can we find a lightweight way to sync daily that works for how you work?' We agreed on a one-sentence async update each morning. I adapted to their style rather than demanding they adapt to mine.
stories.result: The updates started immediately and we caught a timing issue in the authentication flow two weeks before the pilot. The hospital pilot launched successfully. Working with someone whose style differs from yours requires finding the minimum viable sync that gives you what you need without imposing a workflow on them.

stories.title: Very Critical Senior Engineer at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, one senior engineer on the team was deeply critical of junior work in code reviews — technically precise but often blunt to the point of being discouraging.
stories.task: I needed to continue getting useful technical feedback from them without the reviews becoming demoralising.
stories.action: I changed my approach to code reviews with them specifically: I wrote more defensive comments in my PRs pre-empting obvious critiques, and started asking for informal feedback before submitting — 'before I open this PR, does this approach look reasonable to you?' By the time the formal review happened, the big issues were already resolved.
stories.result: The reviews became more collaborative and less adversarial. Their technical depth was genuinely valuable — the friction was about process, not competence. Adapting how you engage with a difficult reviewer can recover most of the value while reducing the friction.

stories.title: Misaligned Priorities in NSBE Officer Team
stories.situation: As NSBE Assistant VP, one officer consistently prioritised events that were interesting to them personally over events that served the most members — creating internal tension about what we should be doing.
stories.task: I needed to redirect their energy without losing their commitment to the chapter.
stories.action: I had a private conversation focused on outcomes rather than preferences: 'Our highest-attendance events are the ones that directly address interview prep and networking. I'd love to find a way to include what you care about — can we design an event that does both?' We co-created an event that incorporated both their interest and a practical skill component.
stories.result: Their engagement increased because they had real ownership. Attendance at their event was strong because it had practical value. Finding the overlap between what someone wants to do and what the organisation needs is more effective than directing them.

question: Tell me about a project that didn't go according to plan.

stories.title: MedStroke ML Accuracy Plateau
stories.situation: MedStroke AI was planned around an 80% ML accuracy target for stroke risk prediction. The project did not go to plan — we plateaued at 45% and couldn't get higher within our timeline and dataset constraints.
stories.task: We had a fixed hospital pilot date, a model that wasn't hitting its target, and a team watching the accuracy number not move.
stories.action: I pivoted the plan explicitly. I reframed the tool's clinical role from 'diagnostic replacement' to 'decision support aid,' adjusted the hospital pilot objectives accordingly, and was transparent with both the team and the hospital contact about where we were and why. I documented the limitation clearly in every piece of clinical-facing material.
stories.result: The pilot ran and reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. The hospital staff adopted the tool because we were honest about its capabilities. The project didn't go to plan technically — but it succeeded clinically because we adapted rather than either overpromised or cancelled.

stories.title: NanoSQL B-Tree Full Rewrite
stories.situation: My NanoSQL project plan included a B-tree implementation in week 3. What actually happened: I finished the B-tree in week 3, shipped it, discovered a data corruption bug under mixed read/write loads in week 4, and did a full rewrite in week 5.
stories.task: The rewrite wasn't in the plan — it cost me the entire buffer I'd built into the timeline.
stories.action: I scrapped the original implementation entirely rather than patching it — the corruption was deep enough that patches would have been unreliable. I rewrote the B-tree test-first: wrote the test suite before the implementation, so I was building toward verified correctness from the start. The rewrite took one week.
stories.result: The final B-tree handled all operations correctly with zero corruption. The project shipped two weeks later than planned. The lesson: test-first development isn't optional for data structures that touch persistent storage — correctness can't be verified by inspection alone.

stories.title: Noon IoT Pipeline Scope Expansion
stories.situation: The Noon IoT pipeline project was scoped for real-time ingestion only. Midway through, the requirements expanded to include historical data replay — a feature that required significant schema changes.
stories.task: The expansion was unplanned and would break our timeline if absorbed without adjustment.
stories.action: I did not absorb it silently. I immediately estimated the new work, presented the timeline impact to my manager, and proposed two options: deliver both features with a revised timeline, or deliver real-time first with replay in the next sprint. I recommended the phased approach with clear reasoning.
stories.result: We went phased. Real-time shipped on schedule with 40% downtime improvement. Replay was delivered in the following sprint. Projects rarely go exactly to plan — the skill is detecting the deviation early and reconfiguring rather than silently absorbing scope.

question: What is something new that you've learned recently?

stories.title: F# Functional Programming at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson I had to learn F# — a functional-first .NET language — to build the event-driven pricing system.
stories.task: F# wasn't a weekend tutorial situation. I was building production systems in it under a real deadline.
stories.action: I spent a focused week on F# async workflows, discriminated unions, and pipeline operators. The biggest conceptual shift was treating data transformation as a series of composable functions rather than mutable state. I built a throwaway prototype to validate my understanding before touching production code.
stories.result: I built and shipped the F# system — aggregating 10K+ pricing records into RESTful endpoints with real-time access to previously disconnected data. More importantly, learning functional programming changed how I think about state management in every language. F# made me a better Python and TypeScript developer.

stories.title: Linux Namespaces and cgroups for Go Container
stories.situation: Building Go Container required learning Linux namespaces and cgroups at the kernel level — documentation that assumes C systems programming knowledge, not Go.
stories.task: I needed to understand how the Linux kernel implements process isolation well enough to use it correctly from Go.
stories.action: I read the Linux man pages for clone(), unshare(), and the cgroups v2 interface. I learned each namespace type independently — PID, mount, UTS — tested each in isolation before combining them. I used strace to observe the system calls my container runtime was making to verify they matched the kernel documentation.
stories.result: I built a working container runtime that correctly isolates processes, enforces resource limits, and maintains filesystem separation. Learning kernel primitives at this level changed how I think about every containerised system I work with. I can now read a Kubernetes pod spec and understand what kernel calls it's generating.

stories.title: React 19 Reconciler Internals
stories.situation: Working on my open source React contribution required understanding the React 19 reconciler — specifically how it handles StrictMode double-invocation in concurrent rendering mode.
stories.task: The reconciler is deliberately underdocumented because it's considered an internal implementation detail. I had to learn it from source code alone.
stories.action: I read the fiber reconciler source systematically — starting from the work loop, through the commit phase, to the StrictMode-specific code paths. I used git-bisection to narrow the relevant changes to specific commits, which reduced the reading surface significantly. I wrote a minimal reproduction case that confirmed my understanding of the mechanism before writing any fix.
stories.result: I can now read React's fiber reconciler and reason about how rendering decisions get made. That understanding is directly applicable to debugging subtle React bugs in production that surface as 'the component renders unexpectedly.' Most engineers debug React by trial and error — understanding the reconciler means debugging from first principles.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to make a decision without all the information you needed.

stories.title: Ericsson Kafka Design Before Specs Finalised
stories.situation: At Ericsson, I had to commit to the Kafka consumer group architecture before the upstream team had finalised the event schema — I didn't know the exact message format or volume distribution across topics.
stories.task: Waiting for the final spec would have cost two weeks. I needed to make a defensible decision with partial information.
stories.action: I identified which decisions were reversible and which weren't. The consumer group topology was reversible — I could reconfigure partition assignment after the fact. The choice of Kafka over REST was not — that required infrastructure commitment. I made the irreversible decisions based on the information I had (volume range estimates), and explicitly flagged the reversible decisions as provisional. I designed the consumer configuration to be tunable via environment variables so I could adjust it without a code deploy.
stories.result: When the final schema arrived, one topic had 3x the volume I'd estimated — I tuned the consumer group via config without a code change. Distinguishing reversible from irreversible decisions is the most important framework for making decisions under uncertainty.

stories.title: MedStroke Hospital Pilot Decision
stories.situation: I had to decide whether to proceed with the Louisiana hospital pilot when the ML model accuracy was at 45% — I didn't have clinical validation data showing whether 45% was medically useful or not.
stories.task: The information I needed — clinical impact data — was only available if I ran the pilot. But running the pilot with a poor tool could damage trust in AI tools with the hospital.
stories.action: I consulted the hospital contact directly rather than making the call alone. I was transparent about the accuracy level, explained what it meant clinically, and asked them to assess acceptability given their workflow. They confirmed 45% was useful as a screening aid when paired with clinical judgment. That was the information I needed to proceed.
stories.result: The pilot ran and reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. Going to the stakeholder directly to get the information I needed — rather than guessing or delaying — was the right move. Sometimes the missing information is a conversation away.

stories.title: Noon Pipeline Architecture with Incomplete Client Specs
stories.situation: At Noon, I had to design the IoT pipeline architecture before the client had finalised their device count and data frequency specifications — two numbers that significantly affected the right technology choice.
stories.task: I couldn't design a scalable system without knowing the scale. But I couldn't wait for specs that might take weeks.
stories.action: I asked for ranges rather than exact numbers. The client gave me 'between 500 and 5000 devices, reporting every 1-5 minutes.' I designed for the high end — 5000 devices, 1-minute frequency — because overprovisioning in an MQTT/Kafka architecture costs almost nothing, while underprovisioning requires a rewrite. I documented the assumption explicitly.
stories.result: The pipeline handled the actual device count comfortably. The documented assumption gave me a clear record of why I made the choice I did — useful when questions came up later. When you're missing information, define the range and design for the hard end.

question: Tell me a time when you linked two or more problems together and identified an underlying issue.

stories.title: Ericsson Latency + Throughput Drop — Same Root Cause
stories.situation: At Ericsson, during load testing, we saw two distinct problems: consumer latency spiking to 380ms (above our 200ms SLA) and throughput dropping by 40% under sustained load. The team initially treated them as separate issues.
stories.task: I suspected they might be related — both appeared simultaneously under the same load conditions.
stories.action: I looked at the timeline of both events in the broker logs and found they were perfectly correlated — every latency spike coincided with a throughput drop. That correlation pointed to a single event causing both. I traced it to consumer group rebalancing: when the consumer group rebalanced, all consumers paused processing momentarily, causing both throughput to drop and latency to spike simultaneously.
stories.result: Fixing the rebalancing frequency — one configuration change — resolved both problems. Latency dropped to under 180ms and throughput stabilised. Treating two symptoms as one underlying problem saved us from solving two problems separately and potentially missing the real cause entirely.

stories.title: Noon Message Loss + PostgreSQL Slowness — Same Root Cause
stories.situation: At Noon, we had two reported issues: intermittent message loss in the IoT pipeline (1-2% of sensor readings missing) and occasional PostgreSQL query slowness under peak load. The team was investigating them separately.
stories.task: I noticed both issues appeared during the same time windows — peak device reporting periods — and suspected a common cause.
stories.action: I added message-level tracing across the pipeline and cross-referenced with the PostgreSQL slow query log. I found that message loss always preceded or coincided with PostgreSQL connection pool exhaustion — when the pool was full, new writes were being dropped silently rather than queued. The 'slowness' was actually timeout behaviour from the same pool exhaustion.
stories.result: Increasing the connection pool size and adding a write queue eliminated both symptoms. Solving the connection pool issue fixed both reported problems simultaneously. Correlation in timing is the first signal that two problems share a root cause.

stories.title: React StrictMode Regression — Two Reported Bugs, One Cause
stories.situation: While working on my React open source contribution, I noticed two separate GitHub issues: one reporting effects firing three times in StrictMode, and another reporting unexpected component remounting in concurrent mode.
stories.task: Both issues had separate reporters and were being tracked independently, but I thought they might share a cause.
stories.action: I read both issue threads carefully and found that both reporters had React 19 with StrictMode enabled. I built a reproduction case that triggered both behaviours simultaneously in the same component tree. Then I git-bisected both to the same commit — a change in how the reconciler scheduled work during StrictMode's double-invoke phase.
stories.result: I linked both issues in GitHub with a comment explaining the shared root cause and a single reproduction case. The maintainers confirmed it was the same regression. My PR targets both fixes together. Finding the common root cause meant one fix instead of two, and a cleaner fix at that.

question: Tell me about a time you made a decision to sacrifice short-term gain for a longer-term goal.

stories.title: Ericsson — Robust Architecture Over Quick Win
stories.situation: At Ericsson, midway through the Kafka microservice build, I identified a shortcut: I could achieve acceptable latency numbers by reducing the number of topics from 6 to 2 and simplifying the consumer group topology. It would have shipped faster.
stories.task: The simplified design would meet the immediate SLA but would make it very hard to add new event types later without a full redesign.
stories.action: I chose the more complex architecture — 6 topics, properly partitioned consumer groups, designed for extensibility. I spent three extra days on it and was transparent with my manager about why. I documented the extensibility rationale so future engineers would understand the decision.
stories.result: The system launched on time (I absorbed the extra days through better prioritisation elsewhere). More importantly, when the team added two new event types after I left, the architecture accommodated them without any structural changes. Short-term gain from the shortcut would have created technical debt that cost more to pay back.

stories.title: Noon — Comprehensive Tests Over Speed
stories.situation: At Noon, I could have shipped the IoT pipeline two days faster by writing only the happy-path unit tests and skipping the comprehensive integration test suite I was planning.
stories.task: Two days mattered — the client demo was close. But the integration tests would catch failure modes that the unit tests couldn't.
stories.action: I chose to write the full 50+ test suite even though it pushed the demo closer. I worked late two nights to absorb the time without slipping the timeline. I was clear with my manager about the tradeoff I was making and why.
stories.result: The tests caught two real bugs — an MQTT reconnection issue and a PostgreSQL write race condition — that would have appeared in production at the worst possible time. The short-term cost was two late nights. The long-term gain was zero production incidents. Comprehensive tests are one of the clearest cases where short-term investment pays back immediately.

stories.title: MedStroke — Hospital Pilot Delay for Accuracy
stories.situation: We could have run the hospital pilot one month earlier with the model at 32% accuracy. Delaying gave us time to improve it to 45% through better feature engineering and class balancing.
stories.task: The earlier pilot would have given us feedback sooner. But I was concerned that a 32% accuracy model in a clinical context would undermine the hospital's trust in AI tools in ways that were hard to recover from.
stories.action: I recommended delaying the pilot by four weeks to improve accuracy and refine the clinical framing. I was explicit that this was a short-term sacrifice — we'd be four weeks behind our original plan — for a longer-term goal of a successful adoption.
stories.result: The pilot ran at 45% accuracy with clear confidence scores and clinical framing. The hospital adopted the tool and reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. A 32% model might have been rejected and closed the door to future AI tools in that hospital. The four-week delay was worth it.

question: How would you respond if you were the last member of the team in the office on a Friday afternoon and the product owner asks you to develop and deploy a change to production?

stories.title: Risk Assessment First
stories.situation: This has a direct parallel to my Ericsson experience — I was the engineer on call for the Kafka pipeline on a Friday when a non-critical configuration change was requested by a stakeholder.
stories.task: My immediate response was to assess the risk before giving any answer, not to automatically say yes or no.
stories.action: I asked three questions: Is this change reversible quickly if something goes wrong? Is there a monitoring system active over the weekend? And — most importantly — is there a business reason this can't wait until Monday when the full team is available? If the answers are 'yes, yes, no' — the change can wait. If the answer to the last question is yes, we assess further.
stories.result: In my specific case, the change could wait. I communicated that clearly to the stakeholder with a concrete Monday morning commitment. They appreciated the clear timeline and honest reasoning more than they would have appreciated a rushed Friday deploy that failed over the weekend.

stories.title: The Responsible Engineer Framework
stories.situation: At Noon, I was once asked to push a quick fix to production on a Friday afternoon because a client was complaining about a UI issue.
stories.task: It was a one-line change — easy to make. But I was the only engineer available, had no one to review it, and if it broke something, I'd be debugging it alone on a Friday evening.
stories.action: I assessed the risk: the fix was in the frontend, the change was small, I had a rollback path, and the client issue was real and blocking them. I made the call to deploy — but I staged it first, smoke-tested it, confirmed the client issue was resolved before fully releasing, and documented the deployment and my reasoning. I also left a clear note for Monday about what had been deployed.
stories.result: The fix worked, the client was unblocked, and Monday the team had full context on what had changed. The key was not refusing by default or complying blindly — it was making a principled risk assessment and deploying responsibly with a rollback plan.

stories.title: When to Push Back Firmly
stories.situation: The scenario where a product owner asks for a Friday production deploy most often represents a planning failure, not a genuine emergency.
stories.task: My job as an engineer isn't just to execute — it's to protect production stability, and sometimes that means pushing back on requests that create unnecessary risk.
stories.action: If the request is driven by genuine urgency — revenue impact, security vulnerability, customer-blocking bug — I proceed carefully: staging environment first, smoke test, rollback plan ready, monitoring active. If the request is driven by impatience or poor planning — I explain the risk clearly and push back. 'Deploying on Friday afternoon with no team backup is how we end up debugging a production outage over the weekend. Here's what we can do Monday morning.'
stories.result: Engineers who can say no to bad deploys with clear reasoning are more valuable than engineers who say yes to everything. The product owner's short-term satisfaction is less important than not creating a production incident on a Friday. Good judgment means protecting the system even when it's uncomfortable.

question: Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

stories.title: The Systems Architect Vision
stories.situation: When I reflect on my trajectory — from building a Kafka microservice at Ericsson to an IoT data pipeline at Noon to a stroke diagnosis system used in a real hospital — I see a pattern: I gravitate toward backend systems that operate at scale and have real-world consequences.
stories.task: My five-year goal is to build on that foundation and grow into a senior engineer who designs distributed systems that other engineers build on top of.
stories.action: In the near term I want to deepen my expertise in distributed systems by working on production systems at companies like this one, where scale is real and the engineering bar is high. I plan to continue contributing to open source — I already have merged PRs in facebook/react and kubernetes-client/java — to stay close to how the best engineers in the industry think. I also want to develop my ability to lead technically: mentoring junior engineers, driving architecture decisions, and translating complex tradeoffs into clear choices for non-technical stakeholders.
stories.result: In five years I see myself as a senior engineer who ships systems that scale to millions of users and raises the technical bar of the teams I work on. This role is a direct step toward that — which is a big part of why I'm here.

stories.title: The Healthcare Tech Track
stories.situation: MedStroke AI, my stroke diagnosis system piloted at a Louisiana hospital, changed how I think about engineering. Watching a clinical tool I built reduce time-to-treatment by 35% made abstract impact concrete in a way that a dashboard metric never could.
stories.task: My five-year vision is shaped by that experience: I want to be an engineer whose work has consequences in the physical world, not just on a screen.
stories.action: I'm pursuing this by staying close to backend and systems engineering — the layer where scale, reliability, and correctness matter most. I want to work in companies building infrastructure that powers critical systems, whether that's healthcare tech, logistics, or fintech. I intend to grow into a technical lead role by being someone who not only ships good code but helps define what good looks like for a team.
stories.result: In five years I expect to be owning end-to-end technical delivery on a complex system and helping a team grow around me. I want to be the engineer people go to when a problem is hard — not just when it's urgent.

stories.title: The Open Source to Industry Bridge
stories.situation: My open source contributions — across facebook/react, apache/kafka, pgjdbc, and kubernetes-client/java — have taught me how elite engineering teams think about API design, backwards compatibility, and system evolution. It's the best engineering school I've found.
stories.task: My five-year goal is to carry that standard into industry and eventually give back to open source at the level of being a maintainer, not just a contributor.
stories.action: I see the next two years as my time to compound professionally — taking on increasingly complex systems problems in a production environment, shipping at scale, and learning from senior engineers who have done it before. By year three or four I want to be the person at a company who bridges the gap between what the team ships and how the broader open source ecosystem evolves. That might mean proposing internal tools as open source, leading adoption of emerging standards, or speaking at a conference.
stories.result: In five years I want my name associated with a system or open source project that other engineers depend on. That's the kind of impact I'm working toward, and companies building at real scale are where that journey happens.

question: What does your best day of work look like?

stories.title: The Kafka Breakthrough Morning
stories.situation: My best days have a consistent shape: a hard problem that has been resisting me, an uninterrupted block of time, and a real user on the other side who needs it solved.
stories.task: The clearest example was during the Ericsson internship when I was tracking a latency spike in the Kafka microservice that only appeared under high-throughput conditions.
stories.action: I blocked my entire morning — no Slack, no standups — and dug into consumer lag metrics, partition rebalancing logs, and JVM GC pauses. By noon I had isolated the issue: one of the six topic partitions was consistently running hot because of uneven key distribution. I rebalanced the partition key strategy, deployed to staging, and watched latency drop from 280ms to under 180ms. I wrote up the root cause clearly so any engineer on the 30-person team could understand it.
stories.result: The system hit sub-200ms consistently after that fix. The best part wasn't the fix itself — it was the deep focus that made it possible. My best day is one where I go deep on something real and come out the other side with clarity.

stories.title: The MedStroke Pilot Day
stories.situation: The day the MedStroke AI system ran its first live session at the Louisiana hospital pilot was the best work day I can remember.
stories.task: I was responsible for making sure the FastAPI backend, the Random Forest model, and the React frontend all ran reliably under real clinical conditions with no support from me in the room.
stories.action: The morning of the pilot I ran a full system check, confirmed the model was serving predictions with correct confidence scores, and walked through the clinical interface one last time with the hospital contact. Then I stepped back and let the tool run. When I saw the first blood sample data come through the system and a prediction appear in under two seconds, I knew it was working. I spent the rest of the day monitoring logs, answering questions, and taking notes on everything the doctors said.
stories.result: Time-to-treatment dropped by 35% in that pilot. My best day is a day when something I built is used by a real person to make a real decision, and it works the way I designed it to. That's the feeling I'm always trying to recreate.

stories.title: The Open Source Merge Notification
stories.situation: The day a PR I'd spent two weeks refining was finally merged into kubernetes-client/java stands out as one of my best work days, even though I was sitting alone at my desk.
stories.task: The contribution fixed a subtle concurrency bug in the informer framework that affected users running high-throughput watch loops. Getting it right required understanding a complex threading model and writing tests that reproduced a race condition reliably.
stories.action: My best day is the one where everything clicks: I read the codebase deeply enough to understand why the bug existed, wrote a fix that didn't introduce new complexity, and got the reproduction test to catch the race on every run.
