The maintainer's final review comment was, 'this is exactly the kind of careful fix this codebase needs.' That sentence made the entire two weeks worth it.
stories.result: The PR was merged and shipped in the next release. My best day looks like this: deep work, a problem that genuinely resisted me, and feedback that tells me I raised the bar. The merge notification at the end is just punctuation.

question: What words would your colleagues use to describe you?

stories.title: The Ericsson 30-Engineer Team
stories.situation: At Ericsson I worked on a 30-engineer team where I was the only intern on several key deliverables — the Kafka microservice, the event-driven pricing system, and the React dashboard.
stories.task: I had to establish credibility quickly, communicate clearly about my progress, and deliver work that full-time engineers could build on.
stories.action: I asked my manager directly for feedback midway through the internship. He said I was reliable — when I said something would be done by Thursday, it was done by Thursday. He also said I was proactive: I had built the React dashboard without being asked, just because I saw engineers wasting time. A peer on the team said I communicated blockers early, which she said was the thing most junior engineers failed at.
stories.result: I believe the words my Ericsson colleagues would use are: reliable, proactive, and a clear communicator. Those three things are what I try hardest to be, because they make the people around me faster.

stories.title: NSBE Chapter Feedback
stories.situation: As NSBE Assistant VP I led a chapter of 300+ members, which meant working with an executive board of 8 people and managing relationships with corporate partners who funded our $3K sponsorship.
stories.task: Leadership at that scale requires different communication than engineering — you have to be consistent, motivating, and honest with people who are giving their time voluntarily.
stories.action: After my term I asked several board members what they'd say about working with me. The word that came up most often was dependable — I showed up to every meeting prepared, I followed through on every commitment I made to the board, and I never passed a problem upward without having thought about a solution first. One member said I was direct but fair, which I took as a high compliment.
stories.result: I think colleagues across both engineering and leadership contexts would say: dependable, direct, and someone who takes problems seriously. I think those are the right qualities to be known for.

stories.title: Open Source Maintainer Feedback
stories.situation: My open source contributions to facebook/react and kubernetes-client/java put me in extended dialogue with maintainers who gave detailed feedback on both my code and how I communicated in PRs.
stories.task: In open source, your communication is your entire presence — there are no in-person cues, no body language, only written clarity.
stories.action: A maintainer on pgjdbc commented that my PR descriptions were unusually thorough — he said most contributors just submit code but I explained the why, not just the what. A kubernetes-client maintainer said I was patient and receptive: when they pushed back on my approach, I engaged with their reasoning instead of defending my first instinct. I take pride in that, because I know how it feels to review a PR from someone who's already decided they're right.
stories.result: I think the word that spans all my contexts is thorough. Whether it's code, a PR description, or a plan for a chapter meeting — I try to do the thinking before I show up. That's what I'd want people to say about me.

question: What is something that you had to push for in your previous projects?

stories.title: Pushing for the React Dashboard at Ericsson
stories.situation: At Ericsson, engineers were spending 60+ minutes per incident tracing events through the Kafka pipeline using raw log files. There was no visual tooling and nobody had been assigned to fix it.
stories.task: I had to push for this project to exist — it wasn't on any sprint board, it wasn't in my official scope, and my initial mention of it got a polite 'we'll think about it' response.
stories.action: I built a working prototype in my own time, brought it to my manager with concrete numbers: 60-minute average debug time, 30 engineers affected, rough calculation of hours saved per week. I framed it not as a nice-to-have but as an engineering productivity investment with a clear ROI. When the first reaction was still hesitation about the timeline, I offered to build it in parallel with my other deliverables and absorb the time myself.
stories.result: My manager gave the green light. The dashboard shipped and cut debugging time from 60 minutes to 10 minutes for the full 30-engineer team. The biggest thing I pushed for in that role was the right to solve a problem I had identified myself. Sometimes the hardest part of initiative is getting the permission to act on it.

stories.title: Pushing for Comprehensive Testing at Noon
stories.situation: At Noon, the IoT pipeline was heading toward launch with only manual testing in place. My manager's position was that 15-20 unit tests were sufficient and we should prioritise delivery speed.
stories.task: I believed that position was wrong for a pipeline handling live sensor data from physical devices, and I had to make that case clearly without undermining my manager.
stories.action: I came to the conversation with two specific examples of edge cases our manual testing had already missed, a timeline showing the extra tests would add only two days to the schedule, and a risk framing that connected test coverage directly to the 40% downtime-reduction goal we were all accountable for. I made it easy to say yes by making the cost concrete and the alternative concrete.
stories.result: I got the two days. I built 50+ tests. Two real bugs were caught — an MQTT reconnection issue and a PostgreSQL write race condition — that would have hit production. Backend reliability improved by 80%. Pushing for the tests was the right call, and having a data-backed case made the push successful.

stories.title: Pushing for a Hospital Pilot Delay on MedStroke
stories.situation: The MedStroke team was ready to run the hospital pilot with our model at 32% accuracy. I was the one advocating for a four-week delay to push accuracy higher and refine the clinical framing of the tool.
stories.task: I had to push against timeline pressure and the team's enthusiasm for getting into the field, because I believed a 32% model in a clinical context would undermine trust in AI tools in ways that were very hard to recover from.
stories.action: I prepared a specific argument: not just 'the accuracy is too low' but what a 32% model would mean in terms of false negatives on stroke diagnosis, what it would look like to a doctor who got the first prediction wrong, and how that experience could close the door on AI tools in that institution permanently. I paired the risk with a concrete plan — four weeks, specific improvements, a milestone accuracy target.
stories.result: The team agreed to delay. We reached 45% accuracy with proper confidence scores. The pilot reduced time-to-treatment by 35% and the hospital adopted the tool. Pushing for the delay was the most important thing I did on that project. The best outcomes sometimes require slowing down.

question: What would you hope to achieve in the first six months after being hired?

stories.title: The 90-Day Production Contribution Plan
stories.situation: When I think about joining a new engineering team, I think about it in three phases: understand, contribute, and extend. Each phase has a different primary activity and a different definition of success.
stories.task: My goal in the first six months is to move through all three phases without skipping the first one, which is the phase most eager engineers rush past.
stories.action: In the first 30 days I focus entirely on understanding: the codebase, the architecture decisions, the testing culture, and the people. I ask questions, I read existing PRs, I sit with the debugging process. In days 30-90 I want to be shipping independently on well-scoped tasks — not the hardest problems, but real production work with no hand-holding. By month six I want to have identified one thing that isn't working as well as it should — a monitoring gap, a testing gap, a slow process — and to have fixed it proactively. That's the kind of contribution that builds long-term credibility.
stories.result: I built this pattern from my internship experiences. At Ericsson I was shipping independently within three weeks and had proposed and built the React dashboard — an initiative that wasn't in my original scope — before the internship ended. I want to replicate that trajectory in a full-time role.

stories.title: The Knowledge Transfer Goal
stories.situation: One of the things I noticed at Ericsson was that tribal knowledge about the Kafka pipeline lived in the heads of three engineers. When any of them were out, debugging slowed to a crawl.
stories.task: In the first six months at a new role I want to be a net contributor to knowledge sharing, not just a consumer of existing knowledge.
stories.action: Concretely, I want to document the systems I touch as I learn them — not in exhaustive write-ups, but in the kind of runbook or annotated diagram that I would have wanted to find when I was trying to understand the system myself. I want to be someone who leaves every codebase slightly more legible than it was before. By month six I also want to have given at least one internal knowledge-share or lightning talk on something I've learned, even if it's small.
stories.result: The React dashboard I built at Ericsson was partly a knowledge-sharing tool — it gave engineers visibility they didn't have before. I want to build that kind of compounding contribution from day one, not wait until I've 'earned' the right to improve things.

stories.title: The First Real Ownership Milestone
stories.situation: I know from my internship experiences that the difference between a junior engineer who grows quickly and one who stagnates is usually ownership — whether they treat their work as something assigned to them or something they're responsible for.
stories.task: In the first six months I want to reach a point where there is at least one system or component where I am the person the team goes to, not the person who routes questions to someone else.
stories.action: I plan to get there by going deeper than the task requires: reading the history of the code I'm touching, understanding why decisions were made, asking about the failure modes that were designed around. At Noon, becoming the go-to person on the IoT pipeline data model happened because I had read every migration and every related PR — not because I'd been there longer than others. I'll do the same thing in a full-time role.
stories.result: By month six I want my manager to be able to say, 'ask Emmanuel about that part of the system.' That's my definition of a successful first six months: real ownership of at least one meaningful piece of the product.

question: If this were your first annual review, what would I be telling you right now?

stories.title: The Ericsson Internship Review
stories.situation: My Ericsson internship ended with a formal evaluation. My manager told me I had exceeded expectations on delivery, communication, and initiative — and he was specific about each.
stories.task: I use that feedback as a benchmark for what I aim to hear in any first-year review.
stories.action: On delivery: I shipped the Kafka microservice processing 400K+ daily events at sub-200ms latency on schedule, and the event-driven pricing system and React dashboard alongside it. On communication: I flagged blockers early, wrote clear PRs, and never surprised my manager with a missed deadline. On initiative: I built the React dashboard without being asked, which he described as one of the most impactful contributions of the internship cycle.
stories.result: He told me I was one of the strongest interns he'd worked with and that the team wanted me back. I hope at a first annual review you'd be telling me something similar: that I shipped reliably, that I made the team around me faster, and that I took ownership of something I wasn't assigned to fix.

stories.title: The MedStroke Leadership Review
stories.situation: MedStroke AI was a project I led from idea to hospital pilot. There was no manager reviewing me — the review was the pilot outcome itself and the team's response to how I led the build.
stories.task: I think the review my teammates would have given me if they were asked was: strong technical execution, good at making hard calls under time pressure, sometimes needed to delegate earlier.
stories.action: I owned the ML pipeline, the FastAPI backend integration, and the clinical framing strategy. I made the call to delay the pilot by four weeks to improve accuracy, which the team initially pushed back on but later credited as the right decision. The area where I grew most was learning to bring the team along on hard decisions rather than presenting them as done deals.
stories.result: In a first annual review at this company, I'd hope you'd say I shipped reliably, I grew in my ability to collaborate on difficult tradeoffs, and I identified at least one problem that wasn't on anyone's radar and fixed it. That's what I'm aiming to earn.

stories.title: The NSBE Leadership Review
stories.situation: As NSBE Assistant VP I ran a chapter of 300+ members and secured $3K in corporate partnerships. At the end of the year the outgoing board gave me informal feedback on how the chapter had grown.
stories.task: The feedback was largely positive but pointed at one real gap: I sometimes over-prepared for meetings to the point where I didn't leave enough room for other voices.
stories.action: I use both the positive and the critical feedback as my template. I hope a first annual review would say: I grew the team's capabilities, I showed up consistently and reliably, and I made the community or product measurably better in at least one concrete way. I'd also want you to be honest if there's something I over-indexed on — over-preparation in my case means I need to work on creating space for the people around me, not just filling it.
stories.result: The honest answer is: I'd want to hear that I delivered on my commitments, raised the bar on something specific, and showed the team I could be trusted with more. And I'd want to hear the one thing I should do differently in year two.

question: What is your biggest strength and area of growth?

stories.title: Strength: Deep Systems Thinking
stories.situation: My biggest strength is that I understand systems at multiple levels of abstraction simultaneously — I can reason about why a distributed system behaves unexpectedly at the protocol level and also explain the business consequence to a non-technical stakeholder.
stories.task: That strength has been most valuable when something breaks in a way nobody anticipated, and someone needs to figure out why quickly.
stories.action: At Ericsson I traced a subtle latency spike to uneven Kafka partition key distribution by reading consumer lag metrics, JVM GC logs, and partition assignment data simultaneously. At Noon I found that two seemingly unrelated production issues — message loss and PostgreSQL slowness — had the same root cause: connection pool exhaustion. In both cases the ability to hold multiple layers of a system in mind at once led to a faster, better fix. I built this strength by working at low levels deliberately: building a container runtime in Go with Linux namespaces and cgroups, and a SQLite clone in C with B-tree indexing.
stories.result: My area of growth is communication speed: sometimes I arrive at a conclusion internally before I've brought others along. I'm working on narrating my reasoning as I go, not just presenting the answer. I've gotten better at this — at Ericsson I wrote the root cause analysis in a shared doc even before the fix was deployed — but it's still my active growth edge.

stories.title: Strength: Bias for Impact
stories.situation: I have a strong bias for work that has a measurable outcome, not just work that is technically interesting. That shapes how I prioritise, what I build, and when I push back.
stories.task: The clearest example is the React dashboard at Ericsson — nobody asked me to build it, but I calculated that 30 engineers losing 60 minutes per incident was a recoverable problem, so I built the thing.
stories.action: I think this strength shows up most in how I frame decisions. When my manager hesitated on the dashboard, I didn't argue that it was a good idea in the abstract — I calculated hours saved per week across the team and turned it into a ROI conversation. When I pushed for the hospital pilot delay on MedStroke, I framed it around what a false negative meant in a clinical context, not just 'the accuracy isn't good enough.'
stories.result: My growth area here is patience: because I'm focused on impact, I sometimes want to move to execution faster than is appropriate when I'm in a new environment and haven't yet built the full context. I've learned from that — the first 30 days in a new role should be dominated by listening, not building. That's a lesson I took from my internship onboarding and now apply deliberately.

stories.title: Strength: Speed of Learning
stories.situation: I learn new technical material very quickly, especially when I can engage with it directly rather than passively. I've consistently moved from zero to productive in new technologies faster than expected.
stories.task: At Ericsson I had never used F# before joining. I needed to be productive in it within days, not weeks, because I was the engineer on a live deliverable.
stories.action: I built a structured approach to learning new technologies: start with the language's official documentation, then read the source code of a project that uses it well, then build a small thing to surface what I don't understand. I used that approach for F# at Ericsson, for MQTT at Noon, and for the React reconciler internals in my open source contributions. It has a consistent track record.
stories.result: My growth area is depth over breadth. I'm naturally drawn to learning across many areas — systems, ML, frontend, open source — and I've had to consciously choose to go deeper in certain areas rather than adding new ones. For the next stage of my career, I'm deliberately concentrating on distributed systems and backend engineering rather than widening my surface area further.

question: What aspects of your work are most often criticized?

stories.title: PR Scope: The kubernetes-client Lesson
stories.situation: The most consistent criticism I've received in my open source work is that my PRs are sometimes too large to review comfortably. A maintainer on kubernetes-client/java told me directly that my PR was too broad to be reviewed safely as a single unit.
stories.task: I disagreed initially — the changes were tightly coupled — but I needed to understand the maintainer's perspective and respond to it constructively.
stories.action: I wrote a detailed comment explaining the dependency chain between the changes. I added inline documentation, added granular commit messages to make the diff easier to follow, and offered a live review call. What I took from the experience wasn't just 'split your PRs' but 'reviewers have limited cognitive bandwidth and your job is to manage it.' I now plan PRs with review ergonomics in mind from the start — even when the changes are logically coupled, there are ways to present them that reduce reviewer load.
stories.result: The PR was merged. I now routinely write a 'review guide' comment at the top of complex PRs explaining the logical order in which to read the changes. That habit came directly from this criticism. I've had maintainers explicitly thank me for it on subsequent contributions.

stories.title: Moving Too Fast in New Environments
stories.situation: At Noon, a senior engineer gave me feedback that I had proposed a major refactor of the MQTT ingestion layer before I had fully understood the constraints that had shaped the original design.
stories.task: He wasn't wrong. I had identified a real inefficiency, but I'd jumped to a solution without knowing why the existing approach existed.
stories.action: I took the feedback seriously. I went back and read the git history, the original design doc, and the ticket that had introduced the current approach. There was a valid reason for it — a specific MQTT broker limitation that the original engineer had worked around. My proposed refactor would have reintroduced a bug that had already been fixed once. I revised my proposal to preserve the workaround while still addressing the inefficiency I'd found.
stories.result: The revised proposal was accepted and improved pipeline throughput. The criticism taught me that in a new codebase, the correct order is: understand fully, then propose. Moving fast in an unfamiliar system is how you confidently reintroduce old bugs. I've built a deliberate 'read the history first' habit since then.

stories.title: Over-Engineering for Current Requirements
stories.situation: On MedStroke, one of my teammates pointed out that the data transformation pipeline I built was significantly more complex than our current data volume required. He wasn't wrong — I had designed it for eventual scale rather than our immediate needs.
stories.task: He raised this as a maintenance concern: if I was the only one who understood the abstraction layers I'd added, that was a problem when I left the project.
stories.action: I simplified two abstraction layers, removed a caching mechanism that wouldn't be needed until we had 100x the current data volume, and documented the simpler version thoroughly. I kept the extensibility points that were genuinely likely to be needed, but removed the ones that were speculative. The exercise taught me to ask 'what is the simplest thing that solves this problem right now, with a clear path to extend it when needed?' before adding complexity.
stories.result: The simplified version was easier to maintain and faster to debug during the hospital pilot. I still think about future extensibility — that's part of being a good engineer — but I now distinguish between extensibility you know you'll need and extensibility you're adding because it feels right. The latter is often the source of this criticism.

question: Tell me about a time you needed information from someone who wasn't responsive.

stories.title: Unblocking the Ericsson Pipeline Design
stories.situation: At Ericsson, the Kafka microservice I was building required specific event schema documentation from a team that owned an upstream pricing service. The schema would determine how I structured my consumer and transformation logic.
stories.task: The upstream team lead hadn't responded to two Slack messages over a week. I had a hard deadline and couldn't proceed without the schema.
stories.action: Instead of sending a third message, I changed my approach. I looked at the upstream service's existing API documentation and source code to infer the schema myself. I wrote out my best interpretation of it, flagged the three fields I was uncertain about, and sent a concise message to the team lead with a specific ask: 'I've inferred the schema below — can you confirm these three fields are correct?' A yes-or-no confirmation is much easier to give than an open-ended request. I also CC'd my manager so the lead knew there was visibility. I got a response within two hours.
stories.result: The schema was confirmed with one small correction. The pipeline design proceeded without further delay. The lesson: frame your ask as a confirmation, not a request for information from scratch. Most unresponsiveness is friction, not refusal — reduce the friction.

stories.title: The Noon Device Spec Blocker
stories.situation: At Noon, I was building the MQTT ingestion layer for the IoT pipeline and needed detailed documentation on the message format and reconnection behaviour of a specific device type. The hardware vendor was the only source of truth and had been slow to respond for two weeks.
stories.task: I couldn't build reliable reconnection handling without knowing the device's exact timeout and retry behaviour, and the vendor contact wasn't responding to the account team's emails.
stories.action: I took a three-pronged approach. First, I set up a test environment with the actual device and ran my own characterisation tests — deliberately triggering disconnects and observing the reconnection pattern in the packet logs. Second, I searched the vendor's public GitHub repositories and found a sample client implementation that revealed their timeout assumptions. Third, I wrote up what I had learned and sent it to the vendor contact with a specific technical question — not 'can you send me the docs' but 'I observed X timeout behaviour in my tests; is this the specified behaviour or a quirk of this firmware version?' The vendor responded within a day.
stories.result: I had the answer I needed and also had first-hand empirical data to validate it. Waiting for unresponsive stakeholders is often optional — you can frequently make progress by gathering your own evidence and then asking a specific question rather than an open one.

stories.title: Open Source Maintainer Stall
stories.situation: During my pgjdbc open source contribution, a PR I had submitted sat unreviewed for three weeks. The relevant maintainer had responded once to say 'I'll look at this soon' and then went quiet.
stories.task: I needed the feedback to know whether my approach was on the right track, and I had already done substantial follow-up work that depended on the direction.
stories.action: I didn't send repeated pings. Instead, I added a concise PR comment that summarised the status: 'Since my last update I've addressed the edge case in the error handler and added two new tests covering the concurrent path. Happy to adjust the approach if anything here seems off — just wanted to flag the update so it's easier to continue where we left off.' I also posted a brief note in the project's contributor Discord asking if there was a better reviewer to tag for JDBC connection pool changes. A second maintainer picked it up the next day.
stories.result: The PR was reviewed and merged within a week of that update. The key was making it as easy as possible to re-engage: a summary of what had changed, a clear statement of what I needed, and a low-friction alternative path to move forward. Pinging is less effective than making the next step obvious.

question: What frustrates you?

stories.title: Preventable Failures Without Process
stories.situation: What frustrates me most is when a failure happens that a clear process would have prevented, and the team's response is to fix the specific failure rather than the process gap that allowed it.
stories.task: At Noon, early in the project there was a production incident caused by a deploy that had no smoke test. It was caught quickly, but the root cause was a missing step in the deployment checklist.
stories.action: I flagged the process gap directly: not as a criticism of the individual who deployed, but as a systemic issue. I proposed a specific, lightweight checklist — three steps, two minutes — and offered to write it up. The team adopted it. What would have frustrated me was if the incident had been treated as a one-time mistake and the process hadn't changed.
stories.result: We had no further deploy-related incidents during my time there. My frustration with preventable failures isn't personal — it's that they're by definition avoidable. I'd rather spend the energy on a process fix than repeat the same fire drill. I try to channel that frustration into a concrete proposal rather than a complaint.

stories.title: Ambiguity Without an Owner
stories.situation: The other thing that genuinely frustrates me is when a problem clearly exists, everyone knows it exists, but there's no owner and therefore no action. I ran into this at Ericsson with the debug tooling gap.
stories.task: Engineers were spending 60 minutes per incident in raw logs. Everyone acknowledged it was painful. Nobody had been assigned to fix it.
stories.action: My response to that frustration is always the same: decide whether I can own the fix myself. If yes, I take it on — which is exactly what I did with the React dashboard. If the fix is outside my scope or requires resources I don't have, I name the gap clearly to whoever can act on it and make a specific proposal, not just a complaint.
stories.result: The dashboard I built cut debugging time from 60 minutes to 10 minutes for 30 engineers. My frustration with ownerless problems is productive when I treat it as a signal to either act or escalate with a clear ask. The one version of frustration I've had to manage is impatience with the pace of change — I've learned that proposing a solution is always faster than waiting for one to appear.

stories.title: Overcommunication as a Substitute for Thought
stories.situation: The frustration I've had to manage most consciously is with communication that happens in place of thinking — long meetings where a document would have been faster, Slack threads that grow because nobody is willing to make a decision.
stories.task: I encountered this most acutely during the MedStroke project when a question about model accuracy thresholds generated a long async thread with ten opinions and no resolution.
stories.action: I ended the thread by writing a concise decision document: the tradeoff in two sentences, the options in three bullets, a recommendation, and a request for a single synchronous decision call. The call took twelve minutes. We had a decision. The thing that had frustrated me — the lack of a decision — was actually a symptom of a process problem, not a people problem. Async threads without a clear owner rarely converge.
stories.result: We moved forward on the model threshold decision and the pilot timeline. I've since learned to recognise when a conversation needs to change form — from async to sync, from discussion to document — and to be the person who makes that call. That's more useful than being frustrated by the length of a thread.

question: What is something 90% of people disagree with you about?

stories.title: Building From Scratch to Learn
stories.situation: Most engineers I talk to think building tools from scratch that already exist — a database engine, a container runtime — is a waste of time when you could just use the existing tool.
stories.task: I hold the opposite view: that building a known system from scratch is one of the most efficient ways to develop deep engineering intuition, and that most engineers who skip this step pay for it later in subtler ways.
stories.action: I built NanoSQL — a SQLite clone in C with B-tree indexing — and a container runtime in Go using Linux namespaces and cgroups. Neither of these will ever be used in production. But when I debug a B-tree-related issue in PostgreSQL, I'm not reasoning by analogy from documentation — I know exactly what a B-tree does under rebalancing because I've written one. When I work with Docker networking, I understand what the runtime is actually doing with namespaces because I've built one. That depth shows up in how fast I debug and how well I design.
stories.result: I think 90% of engineers would say this time would be better spent building something real. My counter is that the real thing you build afterward is better precisely because you built the toy first. I'm not advocating for always reinventing the wheel — I'm advocating for reinventing it once, deliberately, in order to understand it. The engineers I most admire almost all have a version of this in their background.

stories.title: Junior Engineers Should Read Source Code Before Documentation
stories.situation: The conventional advice for learning a new library or framework is: read the docs first, then look at examples, then look at source code if you need to.
stories.task: I've come to believe this order is often wrong for engineers who want to understand systems deeply, and that for complex systems, reading source code early — not late — builds better mental models.
stories.action: When I needed to understand how the React reconciler handled concurrent rendering for my open source contribution, I read the source code before reading any blog posts or tutorials. The source code is the ground truth — blog posts are interpretations of the source code, often written when the version was different. Docs describe the intended behaviour; source code describes the actual behaviour. For debugging and for contributions, the source code is almost always the right starting point. Documentation is for API surface area; source code is for understanding.
stories.result: This view makes most junior engineers uncomfortable because source code is intimidating. But I'd argue the discomfort is part of the value — you can't build a comfortable mental shortcut for something you've never actually read. The engineers I've learned the most from have all been people who went to the source as a first instinct.

stories.title: The Right Time to Push Back is Before, Not After
stories.situation: Most engineers I've worked with tend to raise concerns about a technical decision after it's been made — in code review, in a post-mortem, or in a retrospective.
stories.task: I believe this is a significant engineering failure mode and that the right time to push back on a bad technical decision is always before it's made, even if the social cost feels high.
stories.action: At Ericsson, I pushed back on the initial REST polling architecture before the design was locked in — not because I was confident I was right, but because I had a specific concern and the correct time to surface it was during design, not after we'd shipped a system that would be expensive to change. At MedStroke I pushed for a four-week delay before the hospital pilot, not after a failed one. Both pushbacks required me to be the person in the room saying 'I think we should reconsider.' That's uncomfortable, especially as a junior engineer.
stories.result: Both decisions worked out. The architectural pushback at Ericsson led to a Kafka design that hit sub-200ms latency. The MedStroke delay led to a pilot that reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. I think 90% of engineers are conditioned to defer to decisions that have already been made because changing them is expensive. I believe the real cost is in not pushing back early enough. Being the person who raises the concern before it's a problem is one of the most valuable things a junior engineer can do.

question: Tell me about a time you had to deliver difficult or bad news to someone.

stories.title: MedStroke Model Accuracy Miss
stories.situation: Two days before the MedStroke hospital pilot, I had to tell my team that the ML model's accuracy was 45% — significantly below the 80% internal target I had set publicly at the start of the project.
stories.task: I was the one who had set the target and was the most visibly responsible for the miss. I needed to communicate it clearly, take ownership, and give the team a path forward — not just bad news.
stories.action: I called the team together rather than sending an async message. I led with the number: 45%, not 80%. I did not soften it or add qualifiers before they heard it. I then walked through the root cause: the training data was too small and imbalanced, and I had underestimated the complexity of the feature engineering. I presented three options with honest tradeoffs: delay the pilot, proceed with lower accuracy and different framing, or proceed with 45% and be transparent with the hospital. I recommended the third with a specific clinical reframing — present the tool as decision support, not a diagnosis, and show confidence scores alongside predictions.
stories.result: The team agreed to proceed with the reframing. The pilot ran and reduced time-to-treatment by 35%. The hospital's feedback was that they trusted the tool more because it was honest about its uncertainty. Delivering bad news clearly and paired with a path forward is almost always better than softening it. The team remembers how you handle a miss more than the miss itself.

stories.title: Noon Pipeline Delivery Slip
stories.situation: At Noon, I was three days away from the sprint commitment for the IoT pipeline integration with PostgreSQL when I hit an unexpected schema compatibility issue that I knew would take at least four more days to resolve.
stories.task: I had committed to a delivery date in front of my manager and the downstream team that was counting on the pipeline to start their work. I needed to tell them I was going to miss it.
stories.action: I told my manager and the downstream team lead the same day I knew — not the day the deadline arrived. I was specific: 'I've found a schema compatibility issue that I expect to take four days to resolve. I'll miss Thursday's commitment and I expect to deliver on Monday.' I then offered a workaround: a stubbed data contract they could use to begin their integration while I resolved the real pipeline. I took full ownership of the slip without attributing it to the schema issue — it was my estimation that was wrong, not the schema's fault.
stories.result: The downstream team used the stub and stayed unblocked. The real integration landed Monday, on my revised estimate. My manager later told me the early, honest communication was what he remembered most — not the slip. Delivering bad news early is almost always less damaging than delivering it late. A slip communicated early is a solvable problem; a slip communicated on deadline day is a crisis.

stories.title: NSBE Partnership Rejection
stories.situation: As NSBE Assistant VP I had pitched a mid-sized tech company for a $5K corporate partnership and led the whole process — the pitch, the follow-up meetings, the proposal. The company ultimately declined and allocated the budget elsewhere.
stories.task: I had to tell the chapter leadership, who had been excited about the partnership, that it wasn't happening — and I had to do it in a way that didn't undermine the chapter's confidence or our momentum on other partnership efforts.
stories.action: I brought it to the executive board directly, not through a message. I told them the outcome first, then the reason: the company's budget had been reallocated due to internal priorities, and it wasn't a reflection of the quality of our pitch. I shared what I had learned about their process that would make any future approach more likely to succeed. I then pivoted immediately to what we were doing next: we had two other partnership conversations active and one of them was looking promising.
stories.result: We secured $3K from another company that semester. The board's response to the rejection was stronger because I didn't dwell on it and because I had already framed a path forward before the meeting ended. Delivering bad news well means leaving the room with the team more focused, not less.
